Scraped from home.dartmouth.edu/events · nhhumanities.org · avagallery.org · shakermuseum.org · nugget-theaters.com · entertainmentcinemas.com/lebanon-6

from July 16 to October 14, 2026.

Thursday, July 16, 2026

8:00 am - 8:45 am Thursday Morning Meditation Rollins Chapel

All dates: Jul 16, Jul 23, Jul 30, Aug 6, Aug 13, Aug 20, Aug 27

Each week, we will sit for 25 minutes of guided meditation followed by discussion. Feel free to leave at 8:30 after the meditation or stay for the discussion. Open to students, staff, faculty and community members. No experience needed.

Sponsors: Mindfullness @ Dartmouth, Student Wellness Center, Tucker Center, & Wellness @ Dartmouth.

Questions? Contact the Tucker Center at: tucker.center@dartmouth.edu. 

 

Mindful Dartmouth, Mindful Dartmouth, Mindful Dartmouth, Student Wellness Center, Student Wellness Center, Student Wellness Center, Tucker Center, Wellness at Dartmouth, Wellness at Dartmouth, Wellness at Dartmouth
Full details ↗
Jul 16 Jul 23 Jul 30 Aug 6 Aug 13 Aug 20 Aug 27
8:30 am - 9:30 am Outdoor Yoga Series: Wake Up with Yoga BEMA

Open to students, faculty, & staff. 

Start your day with the birds, the blue sky, and the green leaves in this all-levels mindful movement flow. Each class will open with a brief meditation followed by a gentle, yet energizing, flow that includes sun salutations, strong standing postures, and calming forward folds. We will conclude with a brief relaxation to help send you off on a day built on a foundation of presence and peace.

Bring your mat (or use one of ours), a towel, water and enjoy a gentle entrance to the day.
 

Please RSVP to reserve a spot

 

Student Wellness Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
9 am - 3 pm Course Design Institute Day 2 - July 2026 audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Students-Graduate via Zoom

Dartmouth's Course Design Institute (CDI) provides a foundation in an integrated course design approach that is rooted in student-centered teaching and learning. Participants gain exposure to current theory of course design and, with support from a cohort of peers and guidance from CDI facilitators, apply evidence-based approaches to the designs of their own courses. This process can be used to develop a new course or redesign an existing course. This live institute will take place via Zoom July 14 and 16, 2026 from 9:00 am-3:00 pm.

Apply by June 15!

Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Students-GraduateDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
9:00 am Yoga in the Galleries AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$5–$25

Join our community for an accessible morning yoga practice in the beautiful AVA Gallery space on Thursday mornings at 9 am. Come breathe and move your body through a calming and mindful flow. This class will be taught with options to help guide you in your practice. Please wear stretchy and comfortable clothes and bring a yoga mat and water bottle. Caleigh has been teaching yoga for eight years to students aged 0-100 in a variety of studio and community settings.

Please note: This practice is accessible to those that can get down to the ground and back up without holding onto something, stand unsupported, and can bear weight on hands/wrists and knees.

Classes are open to all. Registration is pay-what-you-can.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
9:30 am - 11:00 am DOCENT INFORMATION SESSION Hood Museum of Art

Do you love art and sharing it with others? Join the Hood Museum of Art’s volunteer docent team! As a docent, you’ll lead engaging and interactive tours for K–12 students and adults. It’s a fantastic way to connect with the community and inspire others. More information about our docent program can be found here.

This docent information session is for community members who are interested in learning more about the docent program. At the event, we will model the teaching methods used on tours, and there will be an opportunity to ask questions and talk with current docents and education staff.

Please register for the information session here.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12:30 pm - 1:30 pm Publishing Talk: Adam Gianelli & Tomás Q. Morin Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

If you've never visited us, this is the event to attend! We'll gather around a large table at the Literary Arts Bridge, Dartmouth's creative writing center, to share lunch and discuss poetry, writing, and publishing with two astonishing poets. The conversation will be moderated by Dartmouth's own Kate Gibbel, whose forthcoming poetry collection, Little Sound, is out this fall.

About the Poets: 

Adam Giannelli is the author of Tremulous Hinge (University of Iowa PressOpens in a new window, 2017), winner of the Iowa Poetry Prize. He is also the translator of a selection of prose poems by Marosa di Giorgio, Diadem (BOA EditionsOpens in a new window, 2012), which was shortlisted for the PEN Award for Poetry in Translation, and the editor of High Lonesome (Oberlin College PressOpens in a new window, 2006), a collection of critical essays on Charles Wright. His writing has appeared in Poetry, Kenyon Review, New England Review, Ploughshares, Atlantic, New York Times Magazine, New Criterion, and elsewhere. His work has been supported by fellowships and residencies from several institutions, including the National Endowment for the Arts, Fulbright Program, James Merrill House, Bread Loaf Writers’ Conference, Sewanee Writers’ Conference, Yaddo, and MacDowell. He has taught at Oberlin College, Hamilton College, Colby College, and Purdue University. 

Tomás Q. Morin is the author of the novel Cat Love and the forthcoming poetry collection My Favorite Things. He is also wrote the memoirs Let Me Count the Ways and Where Are You From: Letters to My Son, as well as the poetry collections Machete, Patient Zero, and A Larger Country. He is coeditor, with Mari L’Esperance, of the anthology Coming Close: Forty Essays on Philip Levine, and a translator of The Heights of Macchu Picchu by Pablo Neruda. He is a fellow of the Guggenheim Foundation and the National Endowment for the Arts. He teaches at Rice University. 

About the Moderator:

About the Katherine Gibbel’s poems have been published in The Iowa Review, jubilat, Second Factory (ugly duckling presse), and elsewhere. Her chapbook Prairie was published by Ethel Press in 2020. She edits and prints Send Me Press, a monthly series of letterpress printed postcard broadsides. She holds an MFA in poetry from the Iowa Writers’ Workshop and lives in Hartland, Vermont. Her first collection, Little Sound, will be published by the University of Chicago Press Phoenix Poets Series in Fall 2026. 

Literary Arts Bridge
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 6:30 pm REAL AND IMAGINED: EXPLORING THE NATURAL WORLD AT THE HOOD AND THE MONTSHIRE Hood Museum of Art, Montshire Museum of Science

THIS EVENT HAS BEEN POSTPONED AND WILL BE RESCHEDULED AT A LATER DATE

This summer the Hood Museum of Art and the Montshire Museum of Science are offering a special workshop together! We will start at the Hood to explore how artists interpret and show reverence for the natural world. We then move to the Montshire to examine and sketch specimens from the collections and think about how the natural world can inspire us creatively. Light refreshments are included, as are all materials. No experience necessary. Participants will need to arrange their own transportation between the Hood Museum in Hanover, NH and the Montshire Museum of Science in Norwich, VT. 

Tickets are $20 per person, or $16 per person for Seniors (65+), Dartmouth students, and Montshire Members. 

Proceeds for this ticketed program support the Montshire. Space is limited. Click here to register.

Photographs may be taken at this event for promotional or educational use. By attending, you consent to be photographed and/or video recorded. Please contact us if you have any questions or concerns.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6 pm - 7 pm 26Xpressions: Common Roots audience: Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-Undergraduate Baker Lawn

Looking to green up your living space but don't quite have a green thumb yet? Join us for Common Roots, an exciting collaborative event presented by OPAL and the Outdoor Programs Office (OPO) as part of Xpressions of Dartmouth 2026. Intentionally designed for beginners, this hands-on workshop breaks down the barriers to gardening and gives anyone interested in house plants a welcoming place to start. You will have the opportunity to take home your very own plant trimmings, and the experts from OPO will be on-site to teach you exactly how to care for these beautiful indigenous plants. To sweeten the evening, we will be serving up a nostalgic favorite: Mud Cups, the classic childhood treat made of chocolate pudding, crushed Oreo "dirt," and gummy worms.

The learning doesn't have to stop when the hour is up, either. To help you connect even deeper with the local environment, OPO will be hosting a follow-up nature walk the very next day to explore these indigenous plants in their natural habitats.

Whether you are a seasoned plant parent or someone who has historically struggled to keep a succulent alive, Common Roots is the perfect opportunity to dig into a new skill, enjoy a sweet treat, and find your community. All materials, plants, and snacks are completely free, and absolutely no prior gardening experience is required — just bring your curiosity and an appetite for fun!

Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-UndergraduateOffice of Pluralism and Leadership (OPAL)
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Oscar Foss Memorial Library 111 South Barnstead Road Barnstead NH 03225

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsOscar Foss Memorial LibraryMelissa Stowell, melissas.ofml@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:45 pm Ex Machina (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Domhnall Gleeson, Oscar Isaac and Alicia Vikander star in this AI thriller, where a young programmer is caught in a lethal game of manipulation between creator and creation.

Caleb Smith (Gleeson), a programmer at an internet-search giant, wins a competition to spend a week at the private mountain estate of the company's brilliant and reclusive CEO, Nathan Bateman (Isaac). Upon his arrival, Caleb learns that Nathan has chosen him to be the human component in a Turing Test—charging him with evaluating the capabilities, and ultimately the consciousness, of Nathan's latest experiment in artificial intelligence. That experiment is Ava (Alicia Vikander), a breathtaking AI whose emotional intelligence proves more sophisticated—and more deceptive—than the two men could have imagined.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Jun 5 – Jul 18, 2026 2026 Juried Exhibition Main Galleries — AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 pm · Free

Presenting the 2026 Juried Exhibition, a biennial open call with no prescribed theme in which artists were invited to submit works of their choice for juror review. The exhibition fills the maine galleries at AVA: Clifford B. West Gallery, Elizabeth Rowland Mayor Gallery, E.N. Wennberg Gallery, and Rebecca Lawrence Gallery Entry.

View the exhibition tour with Juror, Jamie Franklin, Director of Collections and Exhibitions, Bennington Museum, Click here

Click here for the interview with the 2026 Juried Exhibition Juror, Jamie Franklin, Director of Collections and Exhibitions, Bennington Museum. Jamie sits down with AVA's Curatorial Assistant, Peili Heitzman, to discuss his curatorial practice and overall intention for selecting works for the exhibition. June 5, 2026.

From 103 submissions, juror Jamie Franklin, Director of Collections and Exhibitions at the Bennington Museum, selected 54 artists and 62 works for inclusion in the exhibition. Three artists will receive Juror's Recognition Awards, and one photographer will be selected for the Cornelia M. Rahmelow Photography Prize. Award recipients will each receive a cash award and a solo exhibition during AVA's 2027 season.

Selected Artists

Christine Andrews, Nicole Antal, Kate Arslambakova, Emily Battles, Holle Black, Kim Boerner, Nitya Brighenti, Sally Chapman, Jeanne Ciravolo, Gillian Cook, Elizabeth D'Amico, Jennifer Davey, Bonnie Dedrick, Samantha M. Eckert, Alice Eckles, Richard Fedorchak, Dona Mara Friedman, Nicholas Gaffney, Louise Glass, Erik Grau, Marisa Green, Jesse Harrod, Laura Heijn, Craig Jaster, Linda E. Jones, Stephanie Katz, Ann Kremers, Kyle LaPine, Kimberly Largey, Ann Lorenzini, Bridget McAlonan, Marianne McCann, Mary Mead, Lyn Swett Miller, Christopher Nalty, Elizabeth Nelson, Diane Novetsky, Annissa Nugent, Geoffrey Ogenrwot, Travis Paige, Kathryn Peterson, Katie Poore, Kerin Rose, Anne Cogbill Rose, Jonathan Rose, Yasamin Safarzadeh, Carolyn Shattuck, Julia Shirar, Anastasia Tambakis, Mary Teichman, Sheryl Trainor, John Umphlett, Ann Young.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jun 15 – Jul 18, 2026 Interview with Jamie Franklin, Juror of AVA's 2026 Juried Exhibition Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 10:00 am · Free

Interview with the 2026 Juried Exhibition Juror, Jamie Franklin, Director of Collections and Exhibitions, Bennington Museum. Jamie sits down with AVA's Curatorial Assistant, Peili Heitzman, to discuss his curatorial practice and overall intention for selecting works for the exhibition. June 5, 2026.

To view the interview video, Click here

In shaping this exhibition from a large pool of anonymous submissions, I was guided by the sense that diverse practices might enter into dialogue across material, thematic, and emotional registers. Rather than privileging a single narrative, I was interested in how works accumulate into a shifting field of meaning—sometimes discordant, sometimes unexpectedly resonant—reflecting both contemporary instability and enduring forms of attention, care, and curiosity.

Biography | Jamie Franklin received his MFA in Art History from the Williams College Graduate Program in the History of Art. He has been curator at the Bennington Museum since 2005, and the Museum's Director of Collections and Exhibitions since 2020. His scholarship has focused on American art of the early to mid-20th century, with a particular emphasis on the intersection of modernism and self-taught art. He has organized exhibitions and written books, essays and articles featuring artists and topics including Milton Avery, Color Field painting, Erastus Salisbury Field and Alice Neel, Robert Frost's artistic circle, Grassroots Art, Rockwell Kent, Magic Realism, and Anna Mary Robertson "Grandma" Moses. His 2014 exhibition Alice Neel/Erastus Salisbury Field: Painting the People was recognized by the Wall Street Journal as one of the most memorable exhibitions of the year, and his 2016 exhibition Milton Avery’s Vermont was lauded as being “as close to a perfect show as mere mortals can mount.”

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jun 18 – Aug 15, 2026 World Repair: Recent Paintings by John Stomberg Linda Roesch Visual Arts Gallery | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 11:00 am · Free

AVA Gallery and Art Center is pleased to present, "World Repair: Recent Paintings by John Stomberg." From potholes to the justice system, Stomberg sees a world in need of repair. Obsessed with the question "Can we fix the world?", he has now channeled years of artistic inquiry into paintings that explore the ancient Jewish concept of Tikkun Olam, or world repair, through a vivid visual language of sweeping gold lines, expressive geometry, and radiant color. The result of his last few years of exploration are deeply hopeful paintings that ask what it means to mend what is broken, in both the earthly and spiritual sense. To view John's online gallery Click here, all proceeds will be generously donated to AVA!

John Stomberg holds a B.A. in Art History from Georgetown University and a Ph.D. in Art History from Boston University. His distinguished career in museum leadership has included positions at the Boston University Art Gallery, the Williams College Museum of Art, the Mount Holyoke College Art Museum, and most recently as the Virginia Rice Kelsey 1961s Director of the Hood Art Museum at Dartmouth College, from which he will soon retire.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jun 29 – Aug 10, 2026 Call to Artists: A Place Visited Only In Dreams Linda Roesch Visual Arts Gallery, Gallery 3 | AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 am

a place visited only in dreams | call to artists!

Submission Deadline: Mon. Aug. 10. Exhibition dates: Sept. 25-Nov. 14, 2026. Click here to submit your artwork

**Call for Artwork in the Style of Surrealism** A Place Visited Only in Dreams invites artists to explore the shifting terrain of the subconscious, where logic dissolves and imagination reigns. Rooted in the legacy of Surrealism, this exhibition seeks works that construct alternate realities—worlds shaped by dream imagery, memory fragments, intuition, and the irrational. Surrealism has long offered a visual language for expressing what cannot be spoken directly: desire, fear, wonder, longing, and the strange poetry of the mind at rest. In this exhibition, we are drawn to artworks that embrace unexpected juxtapositions, uncanny atmospheres, and illogical pairings that provoke both unease and fascination. Whether playful or haunting, meditative or disruptive, these works invite viewers to step into spaces that feel familiar yet impossible— places visited only in dreams.

We welcome submissions in all media that interpret dream states, altered perception, subconscious narrative, or imagined worlds. Artists are encouraged to consider how their work might open a portal to the irrational, the symbolic, or the deeply personal terrain of inner vision.

Step beyond waking reality. Enter the dream.

Details: Submission Deadline: Mon. Aug. 10. Exhibition dates: Sept. 25-Nov. 14, 2026. Opening Reception: Friday, Sept. 25, 5-7 PM. Artwork Delivery: Tues. Sept. 8, 11-2PM. Artwork Pickup: Tues. Nov. 17. Shipping: UPS only! Must include a paid shipping return label. Must arrive by September 4. Attention: SAM

This is a group, invitational, curated exhibition. Unfortunately, not all submissions will be included in the exhibition, as our galleries have size limitations. Fees are non-refundable and used to support non-profit exhibition programming. Thank you for your submission!

Add to Google Calendar
Jul 7 – Aug 18, 2026 Composition in Oils or Acrylics AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 5:30 pm · $400

Instructor: Susan Gray Bé

Elevate your painting by mastering the core principles of composition while you learn to balance color contrast, pattern, tone, and line. In this class you'll learn from the ground up beginning with a foundational charcoal drawing, before diving into paint to explore advanced techniques like underpainting, wet-into-wet blending, and the luminous effects of glazing.

Click here for the class materials list

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 9 – Aug 27, 2026 Blacksmithing | 6-8:30 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 pm · $75–$575

Instructor: Roff Harroff

In this class students will study the time-honored art of Blacksmithing to make three dimensional objects with iron. Using gas forges, anvils, hammer and tongs students will forge and form iron into sculptural and utilitarian pieces. This class is meant for students ages 16 and up.

8 weeks

$400 + $75 materials fee

Christopher Nolan’s next film, The Odyssey, is a mythic action epic shot across the world using brand new IMAX® film technology. The film brings Homer’s foundational saga to IMAX® film screens for the first time.The Odyssey stars Matt Damon, Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway, Robert Pattinson and Lupita Nyong’o, with Zendaya and Charlize Theron.The Odyssey is produced by Emma Thomas and Christopher Nolan for their company, Syncopy. The executive producer is Thomas Hayslip.LessMore

Rated PG · 1h 55m

In Disney’s live-action adaptation of the beloved Oscar®-nominated animated adventure, Moana (Catherine Lagaʻaia) answers the Ocean’s call and, for the first time, voyages beyond the reef of her island of Motunui with the infamous demigod Maui (Dwayne Johnson) on an unforgettable journey to restore prosperity to her people. The film is directed by Emmy® and Tony Award® winner Thomas Kail (“Hamilton”); produced by Dwayne Johnson, Dany Garcia, Beau Flynn, Hiram Garcia and Lin-Manuel Miranda; and executive produced by Thomas Kail, Scott Sheldon, Charles Newirth and Auliʻi Cravalho, who voiced Moana in the animated features “Moana” and “Moana 2.” Audiences can experience the brilliant cinematic sights, sounds and songs of “Moana” in U.S. theaters on July 10, 2026.LessMore

Rated R · 2h 0m

Evil Dead Burn unleashes the franchise’s most savage and terrifying ride to date, blazing onto big screens with an all-new chapter of carnage and demonic mayhem. After the loss of her husband, a woman seeks solace with her in-laws in their secluded family home. As one by one they are transformed into Deadites—turning the gathering into a family reunion from hell—she comes to discover that the vows she took in life... live on even in death.LessMore

Rated PG-13 · 2h 5m

Before he was the Father of a Nation, he was a soldier fighting to survive. A single misstep thrusts young George Washington into the center of a global conflict, testing his honor, loyalty, and courage. As alliances crumble and the frontier erupts into war, he must confront not only his enemies but the man he's becoming. This is the untold story of Young Washington.LessMore

Rated PG · 1h 42m

The toys are back in Disney and Pixar’s “Toy Story 5,” and this time it’s Toy meets Tech. Buzz Lightyear (voice of Tim Allen), Woody (voice of Tom Hanks), Jessie (voice of Joan Cusack) and the rest of the gang's jobs are challenged when they come face-to-face with Lilypad (voice of Greta Lee), a brand-new tablet device that arrives with her own disruptive ideas about what is best for their kid, Bonnie. Will playtime ever be the same?LessMore

Jul 16 Jul 24 Jul 25 Jul 26 Jul 27 Jul 28 Jul 29

Friday, July 17, 2026

11:00 am Shaker Herb Garden Tour Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

All dates: Jul 17, Jul 31, Aug 7, Aug 14, Aug 21, Aug 28, Sep 4, Sep 11, Sep 18, Sep 25, Oct 2, Oct 9

Weekly Shaker Herb Garden Tour

with Herbalist Happy Griffiths

Fridays at 11 am, meet by the rose bushes in the garden

Join renowned herbalist and founder of the Enfield Shaker Museum Herb Garden, Happy Griffiths, for a guided tour through one of the Museum’s most beloved living exhibits.

Discover the fascinating history of the Shakers’ herb-growing traditions as you explore beds of culinary, medicinal, aromatic, and dye plants. Along the way, Happy will share stories of how these herbs were cultivated and used by the Enfield Shakers, as well as their practical uses in today’s gardens and homes.

With decades of experience as an herbalist, educator, and the visionary behind the Museum’s herb garden, Happy brings each tour to life with engaging insights and a deep appreciation for the connections between people, plants, and history. The Museum’s herb garden features many of the same plants that were grown by the Shakers as part of their thriving herb and medicine enterprise.

Fridays at 11:00 a.m.

Tours are free with Museum admission and are open to visitors of all ages. Whether you’re an avid gardener, curious about herbal traditions, or simply looking for a beautiful way to experience the Museum grounds, this tour offers a unique opportunity to explore the living legacy of the Enfield Shakers.

What to Expect

Tours last approximately one hour and take place rain or shine, except in the case of thunder or lightning. Much of the tour is outdoors in full sun, so we recommend dressing for the weather, wearing comfortable walking shoes, and bringing water, sunscreen, or rain gear as needed.

The herb garden features maintained paths, but visitors should expect some uneven terrain. Benches are available in both sunny and shaded areas throughout the garden for those who would like to sit during the tour.

Visitors are encouraged to engage all of their senses. You’ll have opportunities to see, smell, and ~when invited by your guide~ even taste selected herbs. To help preserve the garden and support the Museum’s educational mission, we ask that visitors do not pick plants or harvest herbs during the tour.

2 pm - 3 pm Watermelon in the Woods: Mindful Nature Walk DOC House, 10 Hilton Field Lane

Add to Google Calendar
7 pm - 10 pm National Theatre in HD: Les Liaisons Dangereuses Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Lesley Manville and Aidan Turner star in this thrilling game of love, lies and social warfare.

Marianne Elliott directs a striking new adaptation of Choderlos de Laclos' classic novel set in the glittering salons of the super-rich, where patriarchy equals power, reputation is everything—and for women, one misstep can mean ruin.

Marquise de Merteuil (Lesley Manville), master in the art of survival, wields her influence with intelligence and control. Alongside the magnetic Vicomte de Valmont (Aidan Turner), they turn seduction into strategy and weaponise desire. But when their alliance collapses into rivalry, the battle between them threatens to destroy everyone in their path.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, July 18, 2026

10:00am Nature Day at the Woodman Woodman Museum 182 Central Ave Dover NH 03820

Join the us for a Free Admission Day to the Woodman Museum in support of our extensive Natural History collections. Various organizations including NH Fish & Game and The City of Dover’s Open Lands Commission will be on our grounds to talk about sustainability and conservation. The Blue Ocean Society will have their 65-ft inflatable whale on the grounds in the morning for whale conservation education. The afternoon will have an hour long animal presentation from Wildlife Encounters.

This event is supported in part by a Civic Life and Belonging Grant from New Hampshire Humanities.

Civic Life and Belonging GrantsWoodman MuseumJonathan Nichols director@woodmanmuseum.org, (603)953-6184
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:30am How the Constitution Helps Us to Disagree Peterborough Town Library 2 Concord Street Peterborough NH 03458

Deliberation is the practice that holds our democracy together, but does it always lead to better decisions? When citizens only talk to like-minded citizens, the thinking gets stuck and the decisions tend to be more extreme. This presentation gives participants a chance to find new ways to think about a contentious issue using the basic principles of the Constitution: freedom of speech and due process. Instead of carving out one’s (predictable) position in the culture wars, we’ll look at the best arguments on a contentious issue from the view point of progressives, conservatives, and libertarians. By the end of the discussion, participants will have a greater understanding of how the Constitution can help us to foster political friendships.

Peterborough Town Library is fully accessible. There is no pre-registration for this event. Please note that library parking is not guaranteed. Patrons who are able are encouraged to take advantage of Peterborough’s walkable downtown by parking in the Depot Square or Riverwalk lots.

Humanities to GoMeg MottPeterborough Town LibraryRebecca Enman renman@peterboroughnh.gov
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:45 pm The Terminator (DFS: Killer Robots!) Loew Auditorium, Black Family Visual Arts Center

Arnold Schwarzenegger embodies the definitive killer robot in James Cameron's masterful 1984 sci-fi action thriller.

Disguised as a human, a cyborg assassin known as a Terminator (Arnold Schwarzenegger) travels from 2029 to 1984 to kill Sarah Connor (Linda Hamilton). Sent to protect Sarah is Kyle Reese (Michael Biehn), who divulges the coming of Skynet, an artificial intelligence system that will spark a nuclear holocaust. Sarah is targeted because Skynet knows that her unborn son will lead the fight against them. With the virtually unstoppable Terminator in hot pursuit, she and Kyle attempt to escape.

The gladiatorial arena is set, with vulnerable flesh and cunning versus a leviathan who totes around massive weapons like so many chic accessories and can rebuild his organs as they get shot away. The pacing and the action are terrific, the future visions of a wasted LA are haunting, and Arnold's performance as one of cinema's greatest villains is so iconic that this character has become shorthand for what we fear artificial intelligence will become.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 18 – Jul 19, 2026 Oval Box Making Workshop Enfield Shaker Museum

$265 – $285

Shaker Oval Box Making Workshop

Saturday, July 18 and Sunday, July 19, 2026

8:30 am – 4:00 pm

Learn how to make your own nest of Shaker oval boxes in our Shaker Oval Box Making Workshop. Guided by experienced box maker John Cashman, you will make five (5) Shaker Oval Nesting Boxes, from Pennsylvania cherry wood. The class will focus on preparing the wood, cutting swallowtail fingers, bending the sides and rims, and applying a finish. These iconic Shaker oval boxes are made with copper tacks and wooden pegs using the same techniques practiced by the Shaker brothers in their workshops.

No previous woodworking experience necessary. Beginners are welcome!

All tools and materials supplied. Students can bring their own eye and ear protection, but we will have plenty of both for everyone. Participants should bring a bag lunch each day and water bottle.

Museum Member – $265/person

Non-Member – $285/person

Class size is limited to 12. Advance registration is required.

All proceeds from this workshop go to support the educational mission of Enfield Shaker Museum.

Coming from a distance? Why not plan to stay overnight in the historic Great Stone Dwelling? Check out room availability here.

Check our Events Calendar for other events happening this weekend.

Want to know more about John Cashman?

John Cashman has been making oval boxes for over 15 years. He’s been an avid student of box making, studying both original Shaker-made boxes and modern production methods. He’s made boxes as small as a quarter, and as large as a coffee table. John sells his boxes through the Shaker museum shops at Enfield, NH and Sabbathday Lake, Maine. He’s recently had a nest of 15 boxes exhibited at The Fuller Craft Museum.

FIFA World Cup 2026 Final Watch Party

Dartmouth Green

Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm 2026 Mary Keane Chapel Summer Concert Series Enfield Shaker Museum

All dates: Jul 19, Aug 9, Aug 16, Aug 23, Sep 13

Mary Keane Chapel Summer Concert Series

Sunday, July 19, 2026

4:00 pm Start

All concerts will be held in the Mary Keane Chapel
Enfield Shaker Museum
447 NH Route 4A
Enfield, NH 03748
shakermuseum.org

Suggested donation $15.00. All concert donations support the ongoing restoration of the organ.

All concerts will be followed by a reception in the Museum Herb Garden (weather permitting) or in the Great Stone Dwelling.

A Capella Chamber Choir

Tessitura

Enfield Shaker Museum and the Brattleboro Music Center are pleased to present the a capella chamber choir, Tessitura on Sunday, July 19.  The concert will feature the music of Monteverdi and Poulenc. Also included in the program will be works by Louis Andriessen, Fenno Heath, and Eric Whitacre.

The choir includes singers from the Upper Valley and the Brattleboro area and is directed by Mark Nelson. A multifaceted musician with wide-ranging musical interests, Mark Nelson has over 30 years’ experience conducting instrumental and choral ensembles.

Organ Restoration Update
Bob Waters and Richard Nickerson are continuing to address important restoration needs on the Casavant organ in the Museum’s Mary Keane Chapel. This fall and winter they removed and completely rebuilt the wind chest that receives and regulates the air pressure produced by the organ’s electric blower. This included replacing all the original leather work that controls the release of air to the organ pipes. In addition to maintaining the organ’s tuning, they have been repairing coupling mechanisms that have failed. Unfortunately the Casavant company no longer makes certain replacement parts, so some aspects of the restoration work requires creative problem solving. We are fortunate to have two craftsmen, well versed in the intricacies of Casavant organ construction, caring for this precious instrument.

Thank you for your support of their ongoing restoration work.

Full details ↗
Jul 19 Aug 9 Aug 16 Aug 23 Sep 13
7:30 pm - 9:00 pm Rhiannon Giddens Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A roots music visionary reclaims the hidden stories of American sound.

Few artists move through musical worlds as boldly as Rhiannon Giddens. A singer, banjo virtuoso and musical storyteller, Giddens brings deep history to life with electrifying performances that connect past and present. From folk traditions to opera, her work shines a light on the overlooked voices that shaped American music and invites audiences to hear those roots in a whole new way.

The peerless Giddens returns to the Hop after leading a sold-out concert in 2022 as Artistic Director of the Silkroad Ensemble.

Get more info here.

Bring friends, touch grass and soak up a free simulcast concert of Rhiannon Giddens on the Green as she performs live in Spaulding Auditorium. Rain location: Top of the Hop

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
3:30 pm - 6:30 pm 26Xpressions: The Way of the Bow – Kyūdō, Expression, and Mindfulness audience: Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-Undergraduate BEMA

Immerse yourself in mindfulness through experiencing Kyūdō (Japanese archery) and the practices of haiku and Shinrin-yoku (forest bathing)!

Kyūdō originated as a Samurai martial art in Japan and evolved into a mindfulness practice. Practicing kyūdō involves relaxing the body, focusing attention, and calming one’s heart/mind for precise drawing of the bow and releasing the arrow. It is a path to opening one's heart and mind to the natural dignity of being human, beyond the obstacles of ambition, frustration, and doubt.  Hitting the target is not considered as important as coordinating body, mind, and bow.

You are invited to interact with members of the local Blue Cliff Kyudojo, led by Ray Chin, including Solomon Diamond D’97 and Belinda Chiu D’98. You can also experience haiku writing alongside poet Keysi Montás, shinrin-yoku (forest bathing), and try Japanese tea and snacks.

Come for part of the time or stay for the entire expression!

Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-UndergraduateOffice of Pluralism and Leadership (OPAL)
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm The Finest Hours: The True Story Behind the U.S. Coast Guard's Most Daring Sea Rescue Campton Historical Society Museum 529 NH Rt 175 Campton NH 03223

On February 18, 1952, an astonishing maritime event began when a ferocious nor'easter split in half a 500-foot long oil tanker, the Pendleton, approximately one mile off the coast of Cape Cod, MA. Incredibly, just 20 miles away, a second oil tanker, the Fort Mercer, also split in half. On both tankers men were trapped on the severed bows and sterns, and all four sections were sinking in 60-foot seas. Thus began a life and death drama of survival, heroism, and a series of tragic mistakes. Of the 84 seamen aboard the tankers, 70 would be rescued and 14 would lose their lives. Michael Tougias, co-author of the book and Disney movie The Finest Hours, uses slides to illustrate the harrowing tale of the rescue efforts amidst towering waves and blinding snow in one of the most dangerous shoals in the world.

Humanities to GoMichael TougiasCampton Historical SocietyNancy Mardin mardinnancy@gmail.com

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, July 21, 2026

All day Century of Civic Sound Exhibit Baker Library, Reiss Hall

All dates: Jul 21, Jul 28, Aug 4, Aug 11, Aug 18, Aug 25, Sep 1, Sep 8, Sep 15, Sep 22, Sep 29, Oct 6

May 19 through October 09, 2026
Baker-Berry Library, Reiss Hall
Curated by Memory Apata, Research & Learning Librarian for Music & Performing Arts
Designed by Max Seidman, Exhibits and Graphic Arts Designer

 

Dartmouth's Soundscape

Generations of community members have shaped Dartmouth's sonic identity through the Baker Bells: from the machinist who hacked player pianos to control the clock tower and the professors' wives who hand-punched hundreds of music rolls, to the student composers writing original works for the tower today. Explore the exhibit and consider: how does sound shape your sense of place? What should Dartmouth sound like and who should decide?

Library

Jul 21 Jul 28 Aug 4 Aug 11 Aug 18 Aug 25 Sep 1 Sep 8 Sep 15 Sep 22 Sep 29 Oct 6
9 am - 3 pm Gen AI Teaching Institute - Day 1 audience: Faculty, Postdoc Zoom

Are you wondering about how to transform your teaching practices in the era of Generative AI? Are you curious about the capabilities of different AI tools? Are you looking for an opportunity to collaborate with other colleagues grappling with the same challenges? This interactive workshop offers a tailored approach to integrating generative AI into your courses. Join us to explore and think through the implications of GenAI for your specific course, and adapt your design accordingly with the support of facilitators and feedback from peers. This is a two-day event (July 21 and July 23).
Apply by June 26

Faculty, PostdocDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation

2:00 pm - 3:30 pm DOCENT INFORMATION SESSION Hood Museum of Art

Do you love art and sharing it with others? Join the Hood Museum of Art’s volunteer docent team! As a docent, you’ll lead engaging and interactive tours for K–12 students and adults. It’s a fantastic way to connect with the community and inspire others. More information about our docent program can be found here.

This information session is for community members who are interested in learning more about the docent program. At these events, we will model the teaching methods used on tours, and there will be an opportunity to ask questions and talk with current docents and education staff.

Please register for the information session here.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Range the Wild Woods Over: Loggers' Songs and Stories Minot-Sleeper Library 35 Pleasant Street Bristol NH 03222

Loggers at the turn of the twentieth century cut the timber that built and warmed our houses and provided the ties for America's ever-expanding railroads. Timber established Portsmouth, Portland and Bangor as important ports, sending New England lumber around the world. Folklorist Jeff Warner relates the songs and stories of the people who worked the wintry woods, showing their humor and their grit, and giving us a glimpse into everyday life in long ago lumber camps. 

Accesible parking lot on the west side of the library. Automatic doors, ramp, accesible restroom.
Humanities to GoJeff WarnerMinot-Sleeper LibraryPatricia Hayward, librarian@bristolnh.gov

7:00 pm - 8:30 pm 26Xpressions: Tasting Tuesday audience: Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-Undergraduate Still North Books & Bar (3 Allen Street)

Join us for a flavorful evening at Still North Books & Bar as we explore the culinary expressions of Italy through storytelling with Italian Program Professor Danielle Callegari. Inspired by her book, A Bite-Sized History of Italy, this interactive Tasting Tuesday invites participants to connect with Prof. Callegari on her work. 

 

Whether you're curious about Italian cuisine, or want to enjoy great food and conversation, this event offers a welcoming space to savor new perspectives, one bite at a time.

Register here on Dartmouth Groups: https://dartmouthgroups.dartmouth.edu/opal/rsvp_boot?id=1630743

Wednesday, July 22, 2026

All day Workshop on Law and Philosophy Provided Upon Registration

All dates: Jul 22, Jul 23

Workshop on Law and Philosophy
Dartmouth College

Wednesday, July 22:
9:30-11:00: Miriam Schoenfield (UT-Austin Philosophy)
with comments from Pacy Yan (Princeton Philosophy / Harvard Law)
Chair: David Plunkett (Dartmouth Philosophy)

11:15-12:45: Daniel Wodak (Penn Philosophy)
with comments from Nate Sharadin (Hong Kong Philosophy)
Chair: Tristram McPherson (OSU Philosophy)

4:30-6:00: Tristram McPherson (OSU Philosophy) and David Plunkett (Philosophy)
with comments from Michelle Dyke (Tufts Philosophy)
Chair: TBD

Thursday, July 23:
9:30-11:00: Chris Lewis (Harvard Law)
with comments from Nico Cornell (Michigan Law)
Chair: TBD

11:15-12:45: Emad Atiq (Cornell Law)
with comments from Kenny Walden (Dartmouth Philosophy)
Chair: Mark Budolson (UT-Austin Philosophy)

4:30-6:00: Lily Hu (Yale Philosophy)
with comments from Susan Brison (Dartmouth Philosophy)
Chair: TBD

Organized by David Plunkett (Dartmouth College, Philosophy)

Co-sponsored by the Dartmouth Philosophy Department, the Leslie Center for the Humanities, the Ethics Institute, the Political Economy Project, and the Rockefeller Center for Public Policy. 

Workshop is free and open to all, space permitting. Please register ahead of time for the papers. Some of the sessions are pre-read, others are not. (The pre-read ones will eventually be marked on the schedule.) People are welcome to only come to part of the workshop, choosing those papers they are most interested in.

Registration: https://forms.gle/Cdhyb8t3bQj4sWuo8

Format for pre-read sessions: 2 min opening remarks, 10 min comments, 5 min response, Q+A.
Format for presentation sessions: 20-30 min presentation, 10 min comments, 5 min response, Q+A.

 

Ethics Institute, Leslie Center for the Humanities, Philosophy Department, Political Economy Project
Full details ↗
Jul 22 Jul 23
9:00 am - 11:30 am AI: Too Artificial? Too Intelligent? Too Much Energy? Lebanon Opera House 51 N Park St, Lebanon, NH 03766

Dan Reicher has spent his career at the intersection of energy, policy, and technology. He served as U.S. Assistant Secretary of Energy, led Google's climate and energy work, co-founded the country's first renewable energy project finance firm, and is currently a Senior Fellow at Dartmouth's own Irving Institute for Energy and Society.

His lecture examines AI from multiple angles: its fast-growing applications, its problematic uses, and — crucially — the massive amounts of energy and water its data centers consume. But Reicher also asks whether AI could become part of the solution, accelerating the clean energy transition by improving how we manage electric grids, industrial systems, and buildings.

NOTE: This lecture is part of a six-lecture series "How Is Artificial Intelligence Transforming America?" Six experts. Six Wednesdays. One of the most important conversations of our time. This July and August, the Osher at Dartmouth Summer Lecture Series brings nationally recognized voices on Artificial Intelligence to Lebanon, NH, and screens worldwide.

Cost:
Osher Member: $35
Non-Member: $45

Save by purchasing the full six-lecture series!
Osher Member: $135
Non-Member: $165

12:00 pm - 12:45 pm Wednesday Virtual Mindfulness Practice Group audience: Alumni, Faculty, Postdoc, Staff, Students-Graduate, Students-Undergraduate Zoom

All dates: Jul 22, Jul 29, Aug 5, Aug 12, Aug 19, Aug 26, Sep 2, Sep 9, Sep 16, Sep 23, Sep 30, Oct 7, Oct 14

Join our weekly virtual mindfulness practice group to deepen your mindfulness and meditation practice while connecting with others in a supportive space. Each session includes a 20–25 minute guided sit followed by a brief discussion about our experiences in meditation and mindfulness practice. An optional reading will be offered each week to help guide or enrich the conversation, though completing it is not required. Open to the entire Dartmouth community; all levels of experience are welcome. Join as often as you can!

Register to receive reminders and links to the optional readings.

Sponsored by: Wellness at Dartmouth, Tucker Center, and the Student Wellness Center.

Jul 22 Jul 29 Aug 5 Aug 12 Aug 19 Aug 26 Sep 2 Sep 9 Sep 16 Sep 23 Sep 30 Oct 7 Oct 14
3 pm - 3 pm Applications due! '82 Upper Valley Community Impact Fellowship (UVCIF) audience: Faculty, Staff, Students-Undergraduate Online Application

Learn more and apply here! The '82 Upper Valley Community Impact Fellowship ('82 UVCIF) enables students to build a long-lasting impact in the place they call home for four years while studying at Dartmouth College. Applications for the current fellowship opening will be reviewed on a rolling basis and are due by Wednesday, July 22 at 3pm.

  • Current fellowship opening (one): partner with the Upper Valley Lake Sunapee Regional Planning Commission! Develop regional labor market predictions, dig into data, and develop recommendations to help decisionmakers prepare now for the future. Fellow to start summer or fall 26.
  • Fellows grow as leaders, develop professional and social impact skills, and learn about the local community.
  • Fellows are paired with a local social impact organization and spend 2-3 on-terms working part-time with the organization and one term working full-time for the organization.
  • Fellows earn over $9,000 in total.

Let's welcome New York Theatre Workshop back to their summer home at the Hop and cheer on the future of innovative theater! Hear from the Pulitzer Prize-winning playwright Lynn Nottage and the boundless Nona Hendryx about their theatrical collaboration in the works.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Hooksett Public Library 31 Mt. St. Mary's Way Hooksett NH 03106

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsHooksett Public LibraryJane Ayotte, jayotte@hooksettlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Hooksett Public Library 31 Mt. St. Mary's Way Hooksett NH 03106

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm Free Outdoor Concert – Beecharmer Enfield Shaker Museum

2026 Free Outdoor Concert Series

BEECHARMER

Wednesday, July 22, 2026

Live music starts at 7 pm. Vending begins at 6 pm.

Join us outside the historic Stone Machine Shop for a FREE concert with Beecharmer!

BEECHARMER is a modern acoustic string band from Wilder, Vermont. A fresh take on a long-time collaboration between husband and wife duo Jes Raymond and Jakob Brietbach, BEECHARMER distills 10 years of international touring into a sound that is part festival and part mountain top. It’s a playful twang with artful arrangements, tight time-polished harmonies, virtuosic fiddle, skillful flat picking, clammer banjo, and Upright Bass. They draw from the deep well of American roots music to combine award-winning songwriting with elements of bluegrass, old-time, jazz and pop. Performing as a duet, or backed by a rotating cast of talented believers, BEECHARMER is a joymaker.

Bring your chairs and blankets and enjoy this free outdoor concert at the Enfield Shaker Museum Stone Machine Shop.

Beer, wine, soda, and snacks will be available to purchase during the concert. (No private alcohol allowed.)

The BOX Food Truck will be on site.

Live music will start at 7 pm. Vending will begin at 6 pm.

Rain or shine (we will move inside in case of rain.) Plenty of free parking.

THANK YOU TO OUR SPONSORS:

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm The Mammoth Road: New Hampshire Folk Tales as an Avenue to Local History and Culture Lake Winnipesaukee Museum 503 Endicott Street North (Route 3) Laconia NH 03247

Folk tales are passed from one generation to another, embellished by time and teller: “Ethan Crawford was as tall as Mount Washington. He could carry at least seven men on his back!” Yet, when we look at folk tales, the people who compiled them and the location from which they spring, we can access history in a new and entertaining way. Join us to hear and explore folk tales collected by the New Hampshire Women’s Federation (1932) and dig into local resources in search of truths. Was it tall tale or town history?

Reservations recommended as seating is limited
Humanities to GoErin MoultonLake Winnipesaukee MuseumBrendan Smith, lakewinnipesaukeemuseum@gmail.com

Thursday, July 23, 2026

9 am - 3 pm Gen AI Teaching Institute - Day 2 audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Public Zoom

Are you wondering about how to transform your teaching practices in the era of Generative AI? Are you curious about the capabilities of different AI tools? Are you looking for an opportunity to collaborate with other colleagues grappling with the same challenges? This interactive workshop offers a tailored approach to integrating generative AI into your courses. Join us to explore and think through the implications of GenAI for your specific course, and adapt your design accordingly with the support of facilitators and feedback from peers. This is a two-day event (July 21 and July 23).
Apply by June 26

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
5:30pm Covered Bridges of New Hampshire - Past and Present Gilford Public Library 31 Potter Hill Rd Gilford NH 03249

New Hampshire was once home to over 300 covered bridges. Today, there are over sixty authentic covered bridges in New Hampshire, forty-six of which are over a century old. These bridges exist solely because of the efforts of a small but powerful community that both recognized their significance and honored their tradition. Join author and photographer Kim Varney Chandler as she shares an overview of covered bridges in the Granite State, along with interesting facts she uncovered while researching her 2022 book, Covered Bridges of New Hampshire. Learn more about both existing and lost covered bridges in your area.

Humanities to GoKim Varney ChandlerPatty Myers Adult Outreach CoordinatorPatty Myers, patty@gilfordlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm - 8:30 pm Hop Film Event: The American Revolution (with Ken Burns in person) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Ken Burns presents Episode Two from his newest documentary series, which examines how America's founding turned the world upside-down. 

Thirteen British colonies on the Atlantic Coast rose in rebellion, won their independence and established a new form of government that radically reshaped the continent and inspired centuries of democratic movements around the globe.

An expansive look at the virtues and contradictions of the war and the birth of the United States of America, the film follows dozens of figures from a wide variety of backgrounds. Through their individual stories, viewers experience the war through the memories of the men and women who experienced it: the rank-and-file Continental soldiers and American militiamen (some of them teenagers), Patriot political and military leaders, British Army officers, American Loyalists, Native soldiers and civilians, enslaved and free African Americans, German soldiers in the British service, French and Spanish allies, and various civilians living in North America, Loyalist as well as Patriot, including many made refugees by the war. 

These stories come to life through reenactments filmed across New England in every season, as well as paintings, letters, lithographs and other archival materials, from museums, galleries and libraries. Narrated by Peter Coyote, The American Revolution includes the first-person voices of nearly 200 individual historic figures, read by a star-studded cast including Meryl Streep, Morgan Freeman, Tom Hanks, Amanda Gorman, Kenneth Branagh and dozens more. The film also uses a wide variety of music, both from the period and newly composed pieces for the series, with recordings by Johnny Gandelsman, Rhiannon Giddens, Yo-Yo Ma and many more.

The Revolution began a movement for people around the world to imagine new and better futures for themselves, their nations and for humanity. It declared American independence with promises that we continue to strive for. The American Revolution opened the door to advance civil liberties and human rights, and it asked questions that we are still trying to answer today.

Discussion follows with filmmaker Ken Burns.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Henry Dearborn - From Captain to Cabinet Secretary Fuller Public Library 29 School Street Hillsboro NH 03244

In this presentation, George Morrison explores the life and career of Henry Dearborn, born in North Hampton, New Hampshire in 1751. He began his career as a young doctor before joining the the Contintental Army, where he served for over eight years. Post-war he served two terms in Congress, later as a cabinet secretary, and was, before retiring in 1824, President Monroe’s Minister to Portugal. But in 1818 he found himself in the middle of a controversy that would extend to 1843. Would you rather be elected governor, or insist on telling the truth? 

Parking across the street. Pre-register with the library 603-464-3595. Handicap accessible in the rear of the building, elevator available.
Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonFuller Public LibraryFuller Public Library, 603-464-3595
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm Banjos, Bones, and Ballads Byron G Merrill Library 10 Buffalo Road Rumney NH 03266

Traditional songs, rich in local history and a sense of place, present the latest news from the distant past. They help us to interpret present-day life with an understanding of the working people who built our country. Tavern songs, banjo tunes, 18th century New England hymns, sailor songs, and humorous stories about traditional singers and their songs highlight this informative program by Jeff Warner. 

Please note the change in date to July 23. 

Our room is fully accessible with plenty of free parking on site.

Humanities to GoJeff WarnerByron G Merrill LibraryKaty Gautsch, katy@rumneylibrary.org

Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Flanders-Osborne Museum 74 Main Street Sunapee NH 03782

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsSunapee Historical SocietyBecky Rylander, 603-763-9872; sunapeehistory@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Corbin’s Animal Garden Madison Library 1895 Village Road Madison NH 03849

In the late 1800s, the banking, railroad, and real estate mogul Austin Corbin returned to his hometown in Newport, New Hampshire. He built a grand estate and bought out his neighbors’ farms to create an 22,000 acre wildlife game preserve stocked with boar, bison, bighorn sheep, antelope, elk, Chinese pheasant, and other imported animals. The grounds eventually became a prestigious private hunting park and hosted illustrious guests including Theodore Roosevelt, the Prince of Wales, Cornelius Vanderbilt, Joe DiMaggio, Rudyard Kipling, and Augustus Saint-Gaudens.

This illustrated slideshow features archival images and a discussion of the complicated history and legacy of New Hampshire’s own American Gilded Age robber baron. The talk will also highlight the important legacy of the role the Corbin family and park naturalist Ernest Baynes played in the saving of the American bison from extinction.

The Chick Room at Madison Library is ADA accessible with ample parking. No registration needed. Light refreshments will be served.
Humanities to GoMary KronenwetterMadison LibrarySloane Jarell, librarian@madison.lib.nh.us
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Hadestown: The Musical Lebanon 6 Cinema
Thu Jul 2311:00a · 7:10p

2h 21m

The live theater capture of the hit musical Hadestown, which won eight Tony Awards in 2019 including Best Musical and the 2020 Grammy Award for Best Musical Theater Album, featuring the five original principals of the Broadway company. The genre-defying new musical blends modern American folk music with New Orleans-inspired jazz to reimagine a sweeping ancient tale. Following two intertwining love stories — that of young dreamers Orpheus and Eurydice, and that of King Hades and his wife Persephon —Hadestown invites audiences on a hell-raising journey to the underworld and back.LessMore

Add to Google Calendar

Friday, July 24, 2026

10:00 am - 11:30 am China, the United States, and the Challenges of a New World Order Osher at Dartmouth 1 Court St. Suite 210 Lebanon, NH 03766

The development of China as an economic and political powerhouse has generated serious concerns in Washington and the West in general. The view that, as a developing nation, China would morph into some sort of capitalist democracy was assumed. This was a profound miscalculation. In fact all the norms of the "Washington Consensus," the western rules-based order, are now being challenged. Join Osher at Dartmouth in Lebanon to examine the evolution of this "rules-based order" driven by western interests and how the dynamics of 3rd world nations (referred to as “the Global South") and China challenge the post World War II western consensus.

Richard Price is a former public relations professional. He has written, published, and presented on a broad range of topics including political, historical, and technical subjects. His decades-long interest in China and the developing world, including his travels to China and Asia, has given him a perspective and deep appreciation of a changing world and the challenges facing western society in coping. He has shared this perspective with many groups, providing insight into evolving geopolitical matters.

COST:
Osher Members - $10
Non-Members - $15

LEARN MORE AND REGISTER

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12:30 pm - 1:30 pm Writing After Dartmouth: Panel Discussion Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Ask a stellar panel of recent Dartmouth alumnae about grad school, writing, publishing, and navigating the years after college. This panel discussion will be moderated by Professor Alexander Chee.

A light lunch will be provided. Registration encouraged. Please email laura.jean.gilloux@dartmouth.edu.

About the Panelists

Carolyn Silverstein ’21 earned her MFA in Creative Writing from Colorado State University and her BA in English Literature and Creative Writing from Dartmouth College. Her writing has appeared in Archipelago, Vol. III, Stonefence Review, the Write Launch, and McNeese Review. Her work has been shortlisted for the Virginia Woolf Award in Short Fiction. A native New Yorker, Carolyn is a certified ski instructor and enjoys spending time with her dog, Gatsby, when she’s not writing.

Allison Hufford ’21 is a graduate of Dartmouth College, where she majored in English literature and minored in environmental studies.  As JRA’s Subsidiary Rights Manager, she oversees the licensing of translation, audio, electronic, book club, and large print rights throughout the world in over fifty languages. She and her team work tirelessly in collaboration with a highly skilled network of foreign agents and scouts to market JRA authors and their work internationally, including in person at the London and Frankfurt book fairs. She is an avid reader with a particular love for character-driven narratives, lyrical writing styles, and anything with a speculative twist.  

Elizabeth Janowski ’21 (she/her) graduated from Dartmouth with a double major in history and film and media studies and served as executive editor of The Dartmouth. She received an M.S. in journalism from Columbia University in 2022. Elizabeth is an assistant editor at Viking Penguin, where she has worked with authors including Amor Towles, Tana French, Alejandro Varela, Ruth Ozeki, Nathaniel Philbrick, and Maggie Stiefvater. Originally from Milwaukee, Elizabeth lives in New York City. 

About the Moderator

Alexander Chee is the best-selling author of the novels The Queen of the Night and Edinburgh, and the essay collection How to Write an Autobiographical Novel. He is a contributing editor at The New Republic, and an editor at large at The Virginia Quarterly Review. He is the recipient of a 2021 Guggenheim Fellowship and a 2021 United States Artists Fellowship, and awards and fellowships from the Whiting Foundation and the NEA, and residency fellowships from the MacDowell Colony, the VCCA, Civitella Ranieri and Amtrak. He is a professor of English and Creative Writing at Dartmouth College and the chair of the Dartmouth Literary Arts Bridge.

Literary Arts Bridge

Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 4:45 pm A SPACE FOR DIALOGUE GALLERY TALK Hood Museum of Art

All dates: Jul 24, Oct 1

I Remember Who I Am When I’m with You

The museum’s Class of 1954 Intern Madeleine Saraisky ’26 will discuss her student-curated exhibition that examines a range of photographs and prints that depict many types of relationships to explore the idea of interpersonal witnessing as a worthwhile action, one that shapes the course of our lives and allows us to impact others in return.

Photographs may be taken at this event for promotional or educational use. By attending, you consent to be photographed and/or video recorded. Please contact us if you have any questions or concerns.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Jul 24 Oct 1
5 pm - 6 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Artist-in-Residence Salon Hopkins Center

Beyond the works presented each Saturday, NYTW invites artists to Dartmouth for a week of studio time. Come hear from these artists-in-residence in an Open Studio setting about the new works they are dreaming up.

Join us in the Morris Recital Hall for the start of the Artist Salons, which will take place throughout the Hopkins Center.

Mystical Tether
Co-created and Composed by Britton Smith
Co-created by Satchel Lee
A new music-theatre work in early development centered on a queer Black grandson and his grandmother. The piece unfolds inside a single house—both literal and psychological—representing belonging, early lessons, comfort and distorted memory.

The Dispute
Created by Michael Breslin
The Dispute is a new musical inspired by Marivaux's 1742 comedy La Dispute. In a fairytale world, a prince and princess enter a drag-out dispute: Who was first to be unfaithful in love, man or woman? A social experiment unfurls. Four young adults, raised in total isolation from birth, are released into the wild to test the origins of love, sex and betrayal. But when one subject's desire starts to outgrow the limits of the experiment, identities and alliances shift, and the whole thing goes dangerously––and gleefully––off the rails.

This event is free but ticketed. Registration is recommended.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7 pm - 9 pm Blade Runner (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Harrison Ford hunts down rogue androids, nearly indistinguishable from humans, in Ridley Scott's masterful 1982 sci-fi noir adaptation of Philip K. Dick's story.

Tasked with hunting down a set of rogue androids, "Blade Runner" Rick Deckard's (Harrison Ford) mission is simple—but the issues it raises are anything but. It covers notions of sentience, empathy and human rights, with iconic supporting performances, unmatched production design and a dreamy synth score. 

One of the most influential films of the past 40 years, a dazzling fusion of retro-noir and future paranoia, set in a decaying, rain-drenched Los Angeles dominated by Mayan pyramids of unimaginable wealth. This "Final Cut" is the most complete version of this undisputed masterpiece, representing 25 years of finessing Scott's vision—a cinematic experience not to be missed.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, July 25, 2026

9:00am Celebrate 250America Kids Day! Moultonborough Public Library 935 Whittier Highway Moultonborough NH 03254

Join us on Saturday, July 25, from 9:00 am to 12:00 pm at the Moultonborough Historical Society for a festive morning celebrating America’s 250th birthday! This family-friendly event features a variety of hands-on activities, including painting wooden spinning tops, crafting paper tricorn hats, and trying your hand at fishing for apples. Step back in time at the historic schoolhouse to practice your alphabet on mini chalkboards, or visit the photo booth to dress up in historical clothing. While you're there, stop by the library station to sign up for Summer Reading and enjoy refreshments like popcorn and mini cupcakes on the museum porch. We can't wait to see you there for this historic community celebration!

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchMoultonborough Public LibraryJosianne Fitzgerald, adultservices@moultonboroughlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:00 am Berry Basket Workshop Enfield Shaker Museum

$65 – $75

Berry Basket Workshop

with Dona Nazarenko

Saturday, July 25, 2026

10:00 – 5:00pm

Join us in the Great Stone Dwelling dining room for a full day workshop!  Berry baskets were traditionally hand woven of oak or ash. They were designed to hold individual servings of berries. A Berry Basket has many uses such as for the gardener, or hobbyist and they also make a great storage container. Participants will use natural reed, paired with a wood handle, and can choose to add color accents if desired.  All skill levels welcome.  This square basket measures approximately 6 ½” x 6 ½” x 11 ½” high with handle.

Who is Doña Nazerenko?

Residing in Tunbridge, Vermont, Doña Nazarenko has been weaving since 1988 and teaching since 1994. While living along the New Jersey coast, Doña developed an interest in woodcrafts and related art forms that developed into her desire to take basket making classes in 1988.

Her exploration of a different art medium has since evolved into a flourishing talent. She now enjoys making traditional baskets using reed with wooden accents to more challenging baskets using birch bark from the area surrounding her current residence in rural Vermont.

The highlight of the early years at her craft was having a one-on-one critique with John McGuire at Peter’s Gallery in 1992. His confidence in her and his discretion continues to help her with her current work. Today she can be seen demonstrating basketry representing Vermont fiber artists at local and out-of-state events. Most days she can be found working in her studio at Country Spirit Baskets

Please bring your own lunch. Water and light refreshments will be provided.

Cost:

Museum Members – $65

Non-Members – $75

All proceeds go to the educational mission of Enfield Shaker Museum.

Coming from a distance? Stay at the historic Great Stone Dwelling. Check our room availability here.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
1:00pm Dublin Public Library 125th Celebration Dublin Public Library 1114 Main Street Dublin NH 03444

On July 25, 2026 we will be hosting a 125th celebration of DPL. A gathering around our towns peace pole will start our ceremony with words from local leader discussing the role of librarians and libraries as a source of illumination. An original musical composition commissioned for DPL's 125th will be performed by Zak Redler. A poetry reading by poet Robert Forbes, then local children will share what the library means to them. There will be a library themed scavenger hunt throughout town. We will then be inviting guests inside for a tour and talk of the library and it's history, as well as a history archive recording booth set up for locals to share stories. We will be making a booklist of the 125 most checked out books for DPL and there will be golden tickets hidden in books to find for prizes. Our staff will unveil a plaque in honor of the Library Directors. Special 125th merchandise that includes limited edition library cards, pins, tshirts, bookmarks and stickers will be available.

This event is supported in part by a Civic Life and Belonging Grant from New Hampshire Humanities.

Civic Life and Belonging GrantsDublin Public LibraryEmaline Bergeron director@dplnh.org

Add to Google Calendar
1:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Marlow Town Library 12 Church St. Marlow NH 03456

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Program to be followed by an Ice Cream Social sponsored by the Marlow Parks and Rec

Humanities to GoMary AdamsMarlow Town LibraryJeanne Kennedy, 603-852-2652
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 5:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Moonie Bentley Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A solo show about lineage, love, motherhood and the grief that comes with it all.

Did you know that although you were carried in your mother's womb, the egg you came from was first formed in your grandmother's womb? Moonie didn't. This year, Moonie turns 30, and has decided to go on dates with 100 men this year. Today, she is going on her 88th. It is not because her Taiwanese mother forced her. Or because she is lonely. Or because she is afraid she won't know how to keep living when her mother dies. 

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

This event is free but tickets are required.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, July 26, 2026

4 pm - 6 pm Her (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

In the near future, a lonely writer develops an unlikely but emotionally resonant relationship with an operating system designed to meet his every need.

Written and directed by Oscar-nominated filmmaker Spike Jonze, Her centers on Theodore Twombly (Joaquin Phoenix), a complex, soulful man who lives in a near-future Los Angeles. 

Twombly, who is heartbroken by the recent end of a long relationship, makes a living writing touching, personal letters for others. He becomes intrigued, however, with a new "advanced operating system" that promises to be intuitive and unique in its own right. After initiating it, he meets "Samantha," a bright female voice (Scarlett Johansson) who proves insightful, sensitive and even funny. Soon, their needs and desires flourish together and their friendship deepens into an eventual love. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4 pm - 9 pm New Energy Summer Summit Irving Institute

Now in its fifth year, the New Energy Summer Summit takes place July 26–29 at the Irving Institute. The Summit brings together early-career energy and climate scholars to increase their capacity to connect across disciplines and sectors to advance equitable and sustainable energy systems.

This year's cohort includes 31 participants, selected from more than 150 applications. Scholars come from all disciplines, including natural science, technology, humanities, art, health and medicine, social sciences, policy and law, and more. 

Over the course of four days, the cohort will engage in a dynamic program of lightning talks, breakout groups, panel discussions, workshops, and networking opportunities.

Learn more about the New Energy Summer Summit

Arthur L. Irving Institute for Energy and Society
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00pm Who Was John Stark? Center Meeting House PO Box 50 Newbury NH 03255

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonCenter Meeting HouseLea McBain, 603-763-5803
