Monday, July 27, 2026

6:00pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Dover Public Library 73 Locust St. Dover NH 03820

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, July 28, 2026

All day Motivational Interviewing 2 Day Training To be determined

All dates: Jul 28, Jul 30

https://forms.gle/5DKZPUcn6VV3pGmV9 (Registration)

Summer 2026 Motivational Interviewing Training—Please register by July 15.

The Dialogue Project: Motivational Interviewing Two-Day Workshop

Tuesday, July 28 and Thursday, July 30
9 a.m.–4:30 p.m. (morning refreshments available at 8:30 a.m.)

 

Location: on campus at Dartmouth College, exact location to be determined

The Student Wellness Center, with support from the Dialogue Project, offers this intensive, interactive (and fun!) two-day workshop aimed at building basic proficiency in Motivational Interviewing (MI). MI is a collaborative communication style for supporting individuals through growth and change.

 

MI trainings are named in Dartmouth's Commitment to Care strategic plan as “effective skill-building trainings that support high-quality mentoring, advising, and teaching.” 

This evidence-based approach has broad application for working with students (and others) and can be an instrumental tool for advisors, coaches, facilitators, and educators. The training is free and open to the entire Dartmouth community. Morning refreshments, snacks, and lunch will be provided.

 

Facilitated by Caitlin Barthelmes, member of the MI Network of Trainers and LB White of the Student Wellness Center. 

 *Please attend both days. We offer this training twice a year. If you can't commit to the full training this time, please plan to attend the event when it is offered in December 2026.

 

Student Wellness Center

Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Maxfield Public Library 8 NH-129 Loudon NH 03307

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsMaxfield Public LibraryJess Charland, jcharland@maxfieldlibrary.org

Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, July 29, 2026

All day 26F DPCS Design Your Own (DYO) Internship Funding Deadline audience: Faculty, Public, Staff, Students-Undergraduate Online Application

Design your own in-person internship experience. Work to support the mission of a remarkable nonprofit, make an impact on an under-resourced community, advance yourself professionally, and receive first-rate mentorship from an alumni mentor. Pick an issue to engage more deeply or a skill you'd like to master and design your internship around that aspiration.

For over 25 years Dartmouth Partners in Community Service (DPCS) has provided funding and mentorship for students wishing to pursue unpaid internships with domestic nonprofits serving under-resourced communities. 

Advising is highly recommended before submitting an application. Advising is highly recommended before submitting an application. Schedule an advising appointment - meet with our staff or student director of internships to build an effective project plan. 

Faculty, Public, Staff, Students-UndergraduateDartmouth Center for Social Impact

9 am - 2 pm Dartmouth Official Gear Store Class of 1953 Commons 104 Paganucci Lounge

This new agreement is designed to simplify the purchasing process, ensure brand consistency, and provide competitive pricing for departments and programs across campus.

To introduce this new service and give you the opportunity to see the quality and range of products firsthand, we invite you to join us at a Vendor Fair on:

Wednesday, July 29

Paganucci Lounge

10 am - 2 pm

At the fair, you will have the chance to:

• Meet the vendor team and learn about available services

• Browse and handle product samples

• Ask questions about ordering, customization, and turnaround times

• Learn how to get started under the new contract

Whether you regularly purchase promotional items, manage apparel orders, or play a role in designing branded merchandise for your department or organization, we encourage you to attend.

More details about the contract and the new ordering process will be shared at the event and in follow-up communications.

Campus Services
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:00am Songs of Emigration: Storytelling Through Traditional Irish Music Blaisdell Memorial Library 129 Stage Road Nottingham NH 03290

Through traditional music Jordan Tirrell-Wysocki relays some of the adventures, misadventures, and emotions experienced by Irish emigrants. The focus is on songs about leaving Ireland, sometimes focusing on the reasons for leaving (a man who is driven from his land by English persecution), sometimes revealing what happened upon arrival (an immigrant drafted into the Union army during the Civil War), and sometimes exploring the universal feeling of homesickness of a stranger in a strange land (a factory worker in London missing his home in County Clare). The presenter discusses the historical context of these songs, interspersing their stories with tunes from Ireland that made their way into New England's musical repertoire, played on his fiddle or guitar. 

Humanities to GoJordan Tirrell-WysockiBlaisdell Memorial LibraryDan Bunker, 603-679-8484
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12:30 pm - 1:30 pm Publishing Talk: Jason Roberts & Peter Orner Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Jason Roberts is an American write of nonfiction and fiction. His most recent book, Every Living Thing, was awarded the 2025 Pulitzer Prize for biography, as well as the PEN America/E.O. Wilson Library Science Writing award. His previous book, A Sense of the World, was a national bestseller, a finalist for the National Book Critics circle Award, and longlisted for the international Guardian First Book Award. He lives in Oakland and Montreal.

Born in Chicago, Peter Orner is the author of eight acclaimed books, including The Gossip Columnist's Daughter; Maggie Brown & Others; Love and Shame and Love; Esther Stories, finalist for the PEN/Hemingway Award; and Am I Alone Here?, a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award. His work has appeared in The New Yorker, the Paris Review, The Believer, and The Best American Short Stories, and received four Pushcart Prizes. A former Guggenheim fellow and recipient of the Rome Prize from the American Academy of Arts and Letters, Orner is professor of Creative Writing at Dartmouth College. He lives with his family in Vermont, where he's also a volunteer firefighter.

Literary Arts Bridge

2:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Bedford Public Library 3 Meetinghouse Road Bedford NH 03110

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

4:30 pm - 6:00 pm Poetry & Prose Reading Series: Jason Roberts Haldeman Hall 41 (Kreindler Conference Hall)

Please join us for a reading with Jason Roberts on Wednesday, July 29 at 4:30 pm in the air-conditioned Haldeman 041, as part of the Cleopatra Mathis Poetry & Prose Reading Series. Jason Roberts is a writer of nonfiction and fiction. His most recent book, Every Living Thing, was awarded the 2025 Pulitzer Prize for biography, as well as the PEN America/E.O. Wilson Literary Science Writing award. His previous book, A Sense of the World, was a national bestseller, a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award, and longlisted for the international Guardian First Book Award. He lives in Oakland and Montreal. The reading will be followed by a Q&A and book signing. Books will be for sale at the event with the Norwich Bookstore. For access needs or with questions, please email english.department@dartmouth.edu.

She is sometimes considered to have been a founder of the United States and is now designated as the first Second Lady and second First Lady of The United States. 6:00 pm Free Outdoor Concert – Never Too Late Enfield Shaker Museum

2026 Free Outdoor Concert Series

Never Too Late

Wednesday, July 29, 2026

Live music starts at 7 pm. Vending begins at 6 pm.

Join us outside the historic Stone Machine Shop for a FREE concert with Never Too Late!

Never Too Late creates Americana and classic folk music performances with rich vocal harmonies accompanied by lively instrumentation.  Whether the performance is an outdoor festival or intimate coffeehouse, Never Too Late fully engages the audience for a fun and memorable experience.  Band members include:

Singer-songwriter Hilde Ojibway creates songs for the band that reflect life’s light and textures; Multi-talented Adam Sorscher stirs with his mandolin, guitar, banjo and vocal harmonies; Outstanding bassist Eric Bronstein marries folk and jazz traditions to ground the band’s sound; and Stunning vocalist Bethany Nafzinger soars with solo performances and heavenly harmonies.

It’s NEVER TOO LATE to share the joy of music!

Bring your chairs and blankets and enjoy this free outdoor concert at the Enfield Shaker Museum Stone Machine Shop.

Beer, wine, soda, and snacks will be available to purchase during the concert. (No private alcohol allowed.)

Faz’s Food Truck will be on site.

Live music will start at 7 pm. Vending will begin at 6 pm.

Rain or shine (we will move inside in case of rain.) Plenty of free parking.

THANK YOU TO OUR SPONSORS:

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30 pm - 7:30 pm LECTURE Howe Library and on Zoom

“Excavations and Exhibitions: Digging into Archeology and the Art Museum”

Hood Museum of Art and Howe Library are collaborating on this year’s national summer reading theme, “Unearth a Story,” with particular reference to paleontology and archeology. The Howe has chosen to read Otherlands: A Journey Through Earth’s Extinct Worlds, by Thomas Halliday. In connection with this theme, Hood Curator of Collections Ashley Offill will explore the instrumental role of archeology in forming many early museum collections and the legacies and lessons of archeological objects in the modern museum with reference to the ongoing exhibitions Stone, Sand, and Clay: Connecting Cultures in the Ancient Mediterranean and From Mastodon to Mosaic: Building an Academic Art Collection in America. This hybrid program is free and open to all. To attend via Zoom, please register here. 

Photographs may be taken at this event for promotional or educational use. By attending, you consent to be photographed and/or video recorded. Please contact us if you have any questions or concerns.

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:30 pm Exhibition on Screen: Turner & Constable Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

This new documentary charts the legacy and rivalry of two of Britain's greatest painters alongside the groundbreaking Tate exhibition.

Born within a year of each other—and celebrating their own 250th along with America—J.M.W. Turner and John Constable both used landscape painting to reflect the changing world around them. Turner's blazing sunsets and sublime scenes from his travels and Constable's idealised depictions of beloved places from home whipped the public of the time into a frenzy of enthusiasm. Constable represents the very best of the old school of realism and pastoral nostalgia; Turner, an exciting new way of depicting emotion and dreamlike impressions. Critics compared their starkly different styles to a clash of 'fire and water.' 

Tate Britain brought these two greats together for a groundbreaking exhibition in London in 2026, and this documentary brings that remarkable showcase of "Rivals & Originals" to the big screen. Discover unexpected sides to both artists with intimate views of sketchbooks and personal items, as leading experts offer insights gleaned from seeing these greats side-by-side, as they so often were in life.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗

7:00pm US@250: You Never Forget Your First Walpole Town Library 48 Main Street Walpole NH 03608

A book discussion of You Never Forget Your First by Alexis Coe. This book takes a fresh look at George Washington, moving beyond the heroic stories many of us learned in school to show him as a real, complicated person. Whether you love history or are just curious to learn something new, this conversation will be welcoming, thoughtful, and easy to enjoy.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. Big WatchWalpole Town LibraryJane Malmberg, 603-756-9806
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Thursday, July 30, 2026

5:30pm Last of the Granite State Rattlesnakes Chocorua Public Library 25 Deer Hill Road Tamworth NH 03817

Rattlesnakes were so common in colonial-era New Hampshire that dozens of places were named for them, ranging from the Massachusetts border to the White Mountains. They were so abundant some settlers could pay their property taxes with the bounties offered for killing them. Today, however, Crotalus horridus, the timber rattlesnake, is the state’s rarest native animal—only a handful are known to survive. State law protects them now, but that may not be enough to save them. Wildlife biologists have predicted this tiny remnant will die out without extraordinary intervention.

How did this feared and fascinating animal come to the very brink of extirpation in our state? This illustrated presentation maps how we got here through the social, political, and natural history of New Hampshire’s rattlesnakes. Historian and former newspaper reporter Dan Billin presents vivid, little-known stories and images from our centuries-long war on our rattlesnakes. Topics include who’s been bitten; the medicinal use of rattlesnake oil by the Shakers and others; the exploits of the “Rattlesnake King of New Hampshire”; the twentieth-century rattlesnake panic in the legislature; and the mysterious recent appearance of rattlesnakes with Pennsylvania genes. This program reviews what is and isn’t being done today to conserve our rattlesnakes, and the many obstacles to their survival.

Humanities to GoDan BillinChocorua Public LibraryShauna Peterson, 603-323-8610
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm What's Your Story? Gay Kimball Library 10 S. Main St Troy NH 03465

This program is a storytelling presentation and workshop that highlights how stories connect us. Presented by Author Veronica Francis, in conjunction with librarian and narrative therapist Erin George, this program will demonstrate connections and commonalities neighbors might not realize they have, despite living nearby for years.

Veronica is a New Hampshire storyteller, keynote speaker, and technology coach who helps people navigate change with clarity, confidence, and a sense of humor. Known for her warm presence and relatable storytelling, Veronica blends real-life experience with practical insight - reminding audiences that adaptability matters more than perfection, especially when the plan falls apart.

This program is free and open to the public, will include refreshments, and will begin promptly at 6 pm. You do not have to be a Troy resident or Gay-Kimball Library patron to participate.

This event is made possible in part by a Civic Life and Belonging Grant from New Hampshire Humanities.

Civic Life and Belonging GrantsGay Kimball LibraryErin George troylibrarynh@gmail.com

Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm Herbal Remedy Making with Maria Noël Groves Chosen Vale Performance Center, Enfield

$70 – $75

Herbal Remedy Making with Maria Noël Groves

Thursday, July 30, 6-8 pm 

In Person at Enfield Shaker Museum, 447 NH Route 4A, Enfield, NH

Join us for Herbal remedy Making with Maria Noel Groves. Your garden is likely at its peak right now and you might be wondering: What can I do with all those wonderful culinary and medicinal plants to enjoy them all winter long? In this remedy demo class, Maria will share her favorite home apothecary techniques to dry herbs and make herbal extracts with fresh or dry plants including alcohol-based tinctures, vinegar’s, glycerites, honey, and oxymel. She’ll demonstrate various techniques, provide recipes, share storage tips, and you’ll have the opportunity to make an extract jar to bring home.
https://wintergreenbotanicals.com/
Maria Noël Groves, RH(AHG), clinical herbalist, runs Wintergreen Botanicals, nestled in the pine forests of New Hampshire. Her business is devoted to education and empowerment via classes, health consultations, and writing with the foundational belief that good health grows in nature. She is the author of the award-winning, best-selling Body into Balance: An Herbal Guide to Holistic Self Care, Grow Your Own Herbal Remedies, Herbal Remedies for Sleep, Herbal Gut Health, and an additional forthcoming book. Learn more about Maria and herbs at WintergreenBotanicals.com.
*All proceeds go to the educational mission of Enfield Shaker Museum
Coming from a distance? Stay in our historic Great Stone Dwelling. Check our room availability here.

Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm Taboo Tour – July 31st Enfield Shaker Museum

$45 – $50

Taboo Tour

at the Enfield Shaker Museum

Step beyond the traditional history tour and explore the darker, more mysterious side of Shaker life. The Enfield Shaker Museum presents a special series of three Taboo Tours, each examining fascinating and controversial topics from Shaker history, including scandal, murder, spiritualism, the afterlife, and the enduring question: Were the Shakers a cult?

Each evening features a unique theme, an interactive hands-on activity, and an opportunity to uncover stories rarely told on standard tours. Guests will also enjoy one complimentary cocktail, beer, or glass of wine with their ticket, along with light refreshments.

Tour Dates:

  • July 31 – 6:00 – 8:00 pm
  • August 21 – 6:00 – 8:00 pm
  • October 30 – 6:00 – 8:00 pm

Cost:

Museum Members – $45 per person

Non-Memvers – $50 per person
Capacity: Limited to 20 participants per tour

Join us for an unforgettable evening of history, mystery, and conversation as we uncover the secrets, beliefs, and controversies hidden within the Enfield Shaker community.

Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:45 pm Summer Film: Sundance Shorts Tour Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A showcase of seven specially curated shorts from the 2026 Sundance Film Festival, the preeminent launchpad for young and new filmmaking talent.

Audiences who crave a bit of that Sundance Film Festival creativity can enjoy a mix of fiction, documentary and animated shorts that are funny, sad, inspiring and full of strong characters. Driven by innovation and experimentation, the Short Film Program seeks out filmmaking's most original voices from around the world. Past alumni of the shorts program include Andrea Arnold, Lake Bell, Damien Chazelle, Jay and Mark Duplass, Debra Granik, Todd Haynes, Dee Rees, Taika Waititi and many others. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Add to Google Calendar
5:00 pm Converse with the Crossroads of the Winds Main Galleries (First Floor), Lebanon

Free

Poet and painter Alice Paalen Rahon (1904–1987) wrote, “I’ve been living in a map on the wall. I think I am at the crossroads of the wind. I converse with it. Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind will bring together a small cohort of artists selected through a competitive open call. How does change (good, bad, or ugly) influence new directions within our studio practice and process?

The exhibition concept supports artists whose practices thrive within ambiguity, inquiry, and material exploration. Thematically, Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind centers on ideas that engages liminal states of being through uncertainty, motion, and environmental or political thresholds. Artists are encouraged to present works that engage with change, approached from various angles of discovery—be it personal perspective, socio-political opinion, cultural memory, or bold and experimental narrative techniques.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 9:15 pm The Matrix (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Keanu Reeves learns kung fu to take on Hugo Weaving and his digital henchmen in this 1999 cyber thriller about the threats and possibilities of an increasingly virtual world.

In retrospect, this 1999 action thriller seems terrifyingly prescient, with its vision of a future where humankind is enslaved by an elaborate digital façade, while a malevolent cyber-intelligence dominates the "real" world. Whoa.

Hacker Neo (Keanu Reeves) takes the red pill and reluctantly joins rebels Morpheus (Laurence Fishburne) and Trinity (Carrie Anne Moss) in their struggle against "the Matrix." But learning kung fu and how to dodge bullets might not be enough against the digital henchmen (Hugo Weaving) determined to protect the system—it might require a completely new understanding of what is "real." 

 Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 31 – Sep 12, 2026 Opening Reception: Friday, July 31, 5-7 PM Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 10:00 am · Free

AVA Gallery and Art Center is honored to present four new exhibitions in the main galleries, July 31 – September 12, Opening Reception, Friday, July 31, 5-7 PM

Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind Brings Together Six Artists Exploring Resilience, Transformation, and the Natural World. Cheryl Betz, Thia Daniels, Marisa Green, Patty Hudak, Carla Kimball, and Anna Prikazchikova — are featured in Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind, a group exhibition examining what it means to remain present and rooted at the intersection of forces beyond our control. The wind does not ask permission before it changes direction. It arrives, shifts the landscape, and moves on, leaving behind evidence of its passing in bent branches, scattered seeds, and the long memory of those who stood in its path. The exhibition spans painting, installation, printmaking, video, and papercut, with each artist approaching themes of cycles, loss, and transformation through a distinct visual language.

If These Feet Could Fly, a collection of paintings by Natasha Wein made entirely with her feet — an act of adaptation, resistance, and devotion born from the experience of living inside a body in decline. In 2019, Natasha Wein was diagnosed with Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, a connective tissue disorder whose multi-system effects and spine injuries made her usual painting practice impossible. She taped paper to the floor and used her feet as brushes. The work continued. Then in 2024, she was struck by a car as a pedestrian, sustaining craniocervical instability and a traumatic brain injury. She now lives with intractable migraine, significantly diminished cognitive capacity, and hours each day spent resting in a dark, quiet room. If These Feet Could Fly emerges from that darkness — and insists on beauty anyway.

Steve Arcone and Talia Roberts: a dual exhibition pairing two artists whose practices, though rooted in different mediums and geographies, converge in a shared devotion to the slow, patient observation of the natural world. Steve Arcone | Icescapes in the Abstract: Images from New Hampshire Ponds From 2021 to 2026, Arcone photographed the fleeting architectures produced by freeze-thaw cycles on small New Hampshire ponds — star-like crystal growths radiating from host twigs, ladder-like mosaics born of crystallographic twinning, melt pools coexisting with intact ice, and complex fractures — all at intimate scales of 8 to 40 centimeters. The macro lens collapses depth and estranges the familiar, merging scientific process with abstract beauty. Organic debris offers occasional clues to context, but the images resist easy identification. Titles drawn from classical and jazz compositions offer starting points; viewers are encouraged to linger and offer what they see. Talia Roberts | Earth Memories, Moon Visions— Roberts collected rock samples from Vermont's Green Mountains, grinding them by hand into pigments and mulling each with walnut oil to produce non-synthetic oil paint whose palette carries the particular gravity of place. The resulting paintings, presented in pairs, turn on a recurring circular motif — an image of the moon — to explore duality: earth and sky, light and dark, memory and vision, the geological and the cosmic. By foregrounding materiality and process, Roberts asks viewers to slow down, to resist visual saturation, and to attend to the quiet beauty held in the natural world. Arcone and Roberts arrive at their subjects by different means, together, these exhibitions become a meditation on scale and looking — bridging the microscopic and the geological, the earthly and the infinite.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 31 – Sep 12, 2026 Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 10:00 am · Free

Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind

Brings Together Six Artists Exploring Resilience, Transformation, and the Natural World

July 31 – September 12 | Clifford B. West and Rebecca Lawrence Gallery

Six artists — Cheryl Betz, Thia Daniels, Marisa Green, Patty Hudak, Carla Kimball, and Anna Prikazchikova — are featured in Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind, a group exhibition examining what it means to remain present and rooted at the intersection of forces beyond our control.

The exhibition spans painting, installation, printmaking, video, and papercut, with each artist approaching themes of cycles, loss, and transformation through a distinct visual language. Together, the six artists propose that making something beautiful in difficult times is not escape but resistance, but a turning toward rather than away. Converse with the Crossroads of the Wind is an invitation to stand at that intersection and listen.

The exhibition opens with a public reception on Friday, July 31, from 5 to 7 p.m. at the Clifford B. West and Rebecca Lawrence Gallery and runs through August 29.

Image credit: Patty Hudak, There Through the Broken Branches, Go, Acrylic on Voile (detail)

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 1, 2026

10:00 am Cyanotypes at Sweetland Farm | 10 am-3 pm Sweetland Farm, Norwich

$25–$180

Instructor: Larissa Burak

This summer join Larissa Burak at Sweetland Farm to explore the historical photographic process of the cyanotype. In this hands-on workshop, students will learn how to mix two-part, iron-based cyanotype chemistry; practice coating techniques; experiment with composition using fresh botanicals from the gardens at Sweetland Farm; gauge exposure times using available sunlight; and develop and oxidize prints.

Students are encouraged to bring additional two-dimensional objects they wish to use in their printing process, including lace, stencils, dried botanicals, and more.

$95+$25 materials fee

Rain Date: Saturday July 18th

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12:00 pm Shaker Herb Garden Tour Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

All dates: Aug 1, Aug 8, Aug 15, Aug 22, Aug 29, Sep 5, Sep 12, Sep 19, Sep 26, Oct 3, Oct 10

Weekly Shaker Herb Garden Tour

with Museum Interpreter Lisa Torres

Saturdays at Noon, meet by the Great Stone Dwelling, outside the Gift Shop Entrance

Join Museum Interpreter Lisa Torres for a guided tour through the Enfield Shaker Museum Herb Garden, one of the Museum’s most beloved living exhibits.

Discover the fascinating history of the Shakers’ herb-growing traditions as you explore beds of culinary, medicinal, aromatic, and dye plants. Along the way, Lisa will share stories of how these herbs were cultivated and used by the Enfield Shakers, while exploring the ecology, botany, and practical uses of the plants growing in the garden today.

A lifelong science educator with a background in environmental chemistry and ecology, Lisa brings curiosity, enthusiasm, and a love of learning to every tour. After a 31-year career teaching science at Lebanon High School, she joined the Museum as an Interpreter and educator, where she enjoys helping visitors discover the rich connections between plants, history, and the Shaker way of life.

The Museum’s herb garden features many of the same plants that were grown by the Shakers as part of their thriving herb and medicine enterprise.

Saturdays at Noon.

Tours are free with Museum admission and are open to visitors of all ages. Whether you’re an avid gardener, curious about herbal traditions, or simply looking for a beautiful way to experience the Museum grounds, this tour offers a unique opportunity to explore the living legacy of the Enfield Shakers.

What to Expect

Tours last approximately one hour and take place rain or shine, except in the case of thunder or lightning. Much of the tour is outdoors in full sun, so we recommend dressing for the weather, wearing comfortable walking shoes, and bringing water, sunscreen, or rain gear as needed.

The herb garden features maintained paths, but visitors should expect some uneven terrain. Benches are available in both sunny and shaded areas throughout the garden for those who would like to sit during the tour.

Visitors are encouraged to engage all of their senses. You’ll have opportunities to see, smell, and ~when invited by your guide~ even taste selected herbs. To help preserve the garden and support the Museum’s educational mission, we ask that visitors do not pick plants or harvest herbs during the tour.

Full details ↗
Aug 1 Aug 8 Aug 15 Aug 22 Aug 29 Sep 5 Sep 12 Sep 19 Sep 26 Oct 3 Oct 10
1:00 pm Eco-Printing: Surface Design with Plants at Sweetland Farm | 1-4 pm Sweetland Farm, Norwich

$25–$125

Instructor: Katie Spencer

Eco-printing, flower hammering, and bundle dyeing are a fun way to transfer color from plants to textiles. In this class, we'll explore both intentional surface designs with plants and also embrace free-form, expressive designs with plants. Sweetland Farm is a great location for an eco-printing class, as they are generously allowing us access to some fresh blooms as well as their compost bins, and both have much to offer the natural dyer looking to make uniquely patterned fabric. Participants will leave the day with a patterned cotton bandana of their own creation & the knowledge to make eco-prints at home.

$60+$25 materials fee

Rain Date: Sunday, August 2

Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 5:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: The Punchline Bentley Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

If we only had a year to live, what would change in us?

The Punchline, a new solo piece from playwright and performer Christopher Rivas (The Real James Bond...Was Dominican), urges us to build an altar to our current life and the moments that touch and change us, while we're still alive. Through humor, story and ceremony, The Punchline reminds us to live in the present and celebrate it as sacred—because nothing can last forever.

World Premiere at City Theatre in Pittsburgh in May 2027

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:30 pm - 9:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Los Feliz Daryl Roth Studio Theater

Two Latina mothers form a bond. Can it withstand the differences that divide them?

Irene and Emilia quickly connect over common ground: they're both Latina, working mothers, against giving soda to toddlers, and could use a helping hand. When Irene hires Emilia as a live-in nanny, Emilia gains Irene as an ally in her immigration process. Housed under one roof, they become company to each other's needs, wants and dreams. As their lives intertwine, can they transcend the divergent realities they were born into and now face?

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

Add to Google Calendar
Aug 1 – Aug 29, 2026 Upper Valley Photographers 4th Annual Open Call Exhibition AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 11:00 am · Free

Call for Entries

The Upper Valley Photographers invite you to participate in the 2026 Third Floor Open Call Exhibition at Gallery 3 @ AVA. This non-juried exhibition is open to all members of the Upper Valley Photographers group and highlights a wide range of photographic styles, techniques, and subject matter.

Whether you’re presenting new work or revisiting pieces from your archive, this exhibition is a great opportunity to connect with fellow photographers and share your work with a broader audience.

(Photo Credit: The Great Salt Plains, OK, by Ellen Jonsson)

Submission Rules:

  • All work must be created by the artist. The exposure that forms the basis of the print must have been made by the artist.
  • Any further manipulation or printing must also be completed by the artist.
  • A maximum of two submissions per artist.
  • Finished work (including mat/frame) must be no smaller than 5”x7” and no larger than 26” wide.
  • All work must be ready to hang (matted or framed).
  • Limited to the first 30 submissions on a first-come, first-served basis. If space is limited, the curator may select only one piece per artist for exhibition.

Artwork Guidelines:

  • Image(s) of your work
  • Title(s)
  • Medium
  • Price (if applicable)
  • Artist statement or brief biography

Important Dates:

Deadline for Submissions: July 10, 2026

Prints Due: July 20–24, 2026 (bring to AVA’s main office)

Exhibition: August 1–29, 2026

Location: Gallery 3 @ AVA (Third Floor), AVA Gallery and Art Center

Entrance Fee: $35 per submission

Submission Link: https://www.artgalleria.com/portal/b37ed07

Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, August 2, 2026

10:00 am Wood Carving: Carved Animals | 10 am-1 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$0–$100

Instructor: Eva Sturm-Gross

In this three-hour hands-on workshop, artist Eva Sturm-Gross will guide participants through carving small animal figures from basswood, using basic hand carving tools such as knives and gauges. Designed to be approachable for beginners, the class covers tool safety, wood selection, and fundamental carving techniques, with an emphasis on hands-on experimentation. Come create in a supportive, focused studio environment where students can work at their own pace and leave with a finished (or well-started) carved animal and deeper appreciation for this traditional craft.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4:00 pm - 6:30 pm NYTW Screening: Hadestown Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

The original cast reunites for this live theater capture of Anaïs Mitchell's Tony-winning musical, partly developed at Dartmouth with the New York Theater Workshop.

Hop Audiences got an early look at Hadestown during the New York Theater Workshop summer residency in 2016, before it went on to win eight Tony Awards, including Best Musical and Best Director for Rachel Chavkin. This live theater capture of that Broadway sensation features the five original principals: Reeve Carney as Orpheus, André De Shields as Hermes, Amber Gray as Persephone, Eva Noblezada as Eurydice and Patrick Page as Hades. 

The genre-defying new musical blends modern American folk music with New Orleans-inspired jazz to reimagine a sweeping ancient tale about the costs of industrialization and how artists can help us bring the world back into balance. Following two intertwining love stories—that of young dreamers Orpheus and Eurydice, and that of King Hades and his wife Persephone—Hadestown invites audiences on a hell-raising journey to the underworld and back.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Add to Google Calendar
5:00 pm Golden Hour in the Garden with Barr Hill Enfield Shaker Museum

$25 – $45

Golden Hour in the Garden with Barr Hill

Sunday, August 2 | 4:00–6:00 PM

The Enfield Shaker Museum is delighted to partner with Barr Hill Gin for a special Bee’s Knees Celebration!

Join us on August 2 from 4:00–6:00 PM for Golden Hour in the Garden with Barr Hill, an afternoon of relaxation, creativity, and flavor. Stroll through the gardens, enjoy the beauty of the season, and experience the important connection between pollinators, plants, and community.

What’s Included

Each ticket purchased includes:

  • One drink ticket for a specialty cocktail or non-alcoholic mocktail featuring lavender syrup from Pumpkin Blossom Farm
  • A curated snack box
  • A handcrafted lavender sachet to take home

Guided Garden Painting Class

Enhance your afternoon by participating in a two-hour guided painting class with local artist Shannon Bender. Shannon will lead participants step-by-step through creating a simple landscape painting. No experience is necessary, and all materials are included.

Painting Class

  • Museum Members $40 per person – Non-Members $45 per person
  • Limited to 16 participants
  • Advance registration required

The garden bar will be open from 4:00–6:00 PM serving specialty cocktails and mocktails. Additional beverages and culinary treats will be available for purchase. Cash and credit cards will be accepted.

A Great Drink. A Greater Purpose.

Barr Hill Gin is committed to supporting pollinator habitats across the country.

Simply post a photo of your cocktail on social media and tag @barrhillgin.

For every tagged post, Barr Hill funds one square foot of new pollinator habitat—tracked, verified, and reported back to participants.

No complicated sign-up. No grand commitment. Just a great drink connected to something that matters.

Come spend a summer afternoon in the garden celebrating creativity, community, and the bees that make it all possible.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, August 3, 2026

9:30 am - 3:00 pm The Poetry of Cinema: a workshop Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

In this workshop, we'll explore the creative crossroads between poetry and cinema, and learn how poets and filmmakers use image, language, and memory to create emotionally resonant work. Drawing on poems, screenplay excerpts, and film clips, we'll examine how a single lyric image can become the foundation for character, scene, and narrative, with a special focus on crafting the opening pages of a screenplay. Whether you're interested in writing poetry, screenplays, or both, you'll leave with practical strategies for getting started, shaping your first pages, and approaching revision with fresh eyes.Visiting Poets: John Wall Barger, Elena Karina Byrne, and Perry Janes | Moderator: Vievee Francis

SCHEDULE
9:30-10:30 AM - John Wall Barger

11:00 AM-12:00 PM - Elena Karina Byrne

12:00-1:30 PM - Lunch provided. (Please email Laura Jean Gilloux with dietary needs.)

1:30-3:00 PM - Perry Janes

John Wall Barger is the author of seven books of poems and a collection of essays. His latest book of poems, Resurrection Pie, just came out with LSU Press. He’s a contract editor for Frontenac House, lives in Vermont, and lectures in the Writing Program at Dartmouth College.

Elena Karina Byrne is a screenwriter, essayist, reviewer, multi-media artist, and editor. She also works as a freelance lecturer and national arts programs curator. Author of five collections of poetry including If This Makes You Nervous (Omnidawn), No, Don’t (What Books Press), Squander (Omnidawn), MASQUE (Tupelo Press), and The Flammable Bird (Zoo Press/Tupelo Press), Elena is a Pushcart Prize and Best American Poetry recipient. Her poems, reviews, essays, and interviews can be found in POETRY, The Kenyon Review, The Paris Review, Poetry International, Poetry Daily, Ploughshares, BOMB, Academy of American Poets Poem-A-Day, and elsewhere.

Perry Janes is the author of the poetry collection FIND ME WHEN YOU’RE READY (Curbstone Books/Northwestern University Press, 2024), winner of the Eric Hoffer Award for Poetry. A recipient of the Pushcart Prize, his poems and short stories have appeared in POETRY, Electric Literature, Poem-a-Day, Best New Poets, The Threepenny Review, Zyzzyva, The Michigan Quarterly Review, Subtropics, Prairie Schooner, Beloit Poetry Journal, The Adroit Journal, The Missouri Review, and others. He earned his MFA in Poetry from Warren Wilson College, and his BA from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, where he was a 5-time recipient of the Hopwood Award.

Vievee Francis is the author of five books of poetry: Blue-Tail Fly (2007), Horse in the Dark (2010), Forest Primeval (2016), The Shared World (2023), and the forthcoming Cleaning the Houses of the Dead. Born in West Texas and raised in Metropolitan Detroit, Francis has received numerous awards and recognition for her work including a Guggenheim Fellowship (2024), a Vermont Book Award (2023), the Aiken Taylor Award for Modern American Poetry (2021), the Kingsley Tufts Poetry Award (2017), and the Hurston Wright Legacy Award (2016). Francis, along with composer Jonathan Berger and artist Enrico Riley, wrote the libretto for the transdisciplinary opera The Ritual of Breath which premiered in 2022. She has served as an Associate Editor for Callaloo and currently holds the Burlington Northern Foundation Professorship in Creative Writing at Dartmouth College.

Literary Arts Bridge

10:00 am Storytime in the Garden Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

Free

Storytime in the Garden

with the Enfield Public Library

Monday, August 3 @ 10 am

Join us for a special outdoor Storytime in the Garden in partnership with the Enfield Public Library at the Enfield Shaker Museum Garden.

On Monday, August 3 at 10:00 a.m., librarian Miss Kate will lead stories and songs for children under 5 and their caregivers in the beautiful surroundings of the Enfield Shaker Museum Children’s Garden.

After storytime, children will create a simple garden-themed ornament to take home, then explore our new Touch, See, Smell Sensory Garden and Children’s Pollinator Garden, where they can discover plants, flowers, and pollinators through hands-on exploration.

This event is free and open to the public, and all families are welcome. We hope you’ll join us for a morning of stories, creativity, and garden fun!

Rain Location: School room in the Ministry House

Aug 3 – Aug 7, 2026 ages 12-16: Figurative Carving Intensive | 9 am-12 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 9:00 am · $0–$281

1 week | August 3-10 | 9 am-12 pm

Instructor: Eva Sturm-Gross

In this course students will learn how to carve wood by hand safely and effectively. This class is designed to not only teach students the mechanics of carving but to learn to think three dimensionally in sculpture. We will work with a variety of hand carving tools and learn how to sharpen and maintain them. Students will start by carving small animal figures before designing and executing their own project.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Aug 3 – Aug 7, 2026 Middle Schoolers: Forging and Other Life Skills | 9:30 am-3:30 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 9:30 am · $0–$595

M-F, Aug 3-7 | 9:30 am-3:30 pm

Instructor: Sabrina Fadial

In these interactive, experiential classes, middle school-aged campers will make, experiment, and problem-solve with their hands. Over one week, students will transform raw metal into finished objects, building technical skills alongside resilience, teamwork, and self-confidence.

*This class is designed for 5th-8th graders (must have graduated from 5th grade).

This class meets in AVA's metal studio.

Tiered Pricing: $595, $480, $245, or pay-what-you-can

Sabrina Fadial is an artist, educator, and mentor with a decades-long professional art practice and over 30 years of experience teaching and working with young people. Her work as a sculptor and metals artist informs her dynamic, hands-on teaching approach that centers experiential learning, curiosity, and creative problem-solving.

Sabrina has taught art, design, welding, metals, and blacksmithing to students from middle school through graduate school in schools, museums, afterschool programs, and community organizations. Deeply committed to youth empowerment—especially underserved communities, inner-city students, and girls and female+ students—she creates collaborative learning environments where students build technical skills, confidence, and resilience through making.

She currently serves as Executive Director of the T.W. Wood Museum in Montpelier while continuing to teach and mentor young people in welding and metal arts. Her commitment to access and equity aligns perfectly with AVA's inclusionary pricing approach.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, August 4, 2026

9 am - 12 pm New Faculty Orientation to Teaching audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Students-Graduate Zoom

Dartmouth's New Faculty Orientation to Teaching provides useful information and introductions to help you get started successfully in your teaching role at Dartmouth. Facilitated by the Dartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL) and Learning Design & Innovation (LDI), the Orientation to Teaching is designed to complement other welcome and orientation sessions (with your department, the Dean of Faculty, and Office of Human Resources) that you might attend as you begin your work at the College. At Dartmouth's New Faculty Orientation to Teaching, participants will:

  • Discover resources and support for teaching
  • Hear from faculty and students about teaching and learning at Dartmouth
  • Meet people who can support your teaching
  • Meet other new faculty 
  • Get excited and more relaxed about teaching at Dartmouth This event is intended for faculty new to Dartmouth each year, or who will begin teaching at Dartmouth in the academic year.

If you are unsure whether this event is suitable for you, please contact us to inquire at dcal@dartmouth.edu. This orientation will take place on Zoom. Please register to receive meeting details.

Register here. (opens new tab)

Faculty, Postdoc, Students-GraduateDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation

5:00 pm - 6:30 pm Poetry Reading: Elena Karina Byrne & Perry Janes Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Elena Karina Byrne is a screenwriter, essayist, reviewer, multi-media artist, and editor. She also works as a freelance lecturer and national arts programs curator. Author of five collections of poetry including If This Makes You Nervous (Omnidawn), No, Don’t (What Books Press), Squander (Omnidawn), MASQUE (Tupelo Press), and The Flammable Bird (Zoo Press/Tupelo Press), Elena is a Pushcart Prize and Best American Poetry recipient. Her poems, reviews, essays, and interviews can be found in POETRY, The Kenyon Review, The Paris Review, Poetry International, Poetry Daily, Ploughshares, BOMB, Academy of American Poets Poem-A-Day, and elsewhere.

Perry Janes is the author of the poetry collection FIND ME WHEN YOU’RE READY (Curbstone Books/Northwestern University Press, 2024), winner of the Eric Hoffer Award for Poetry. A recipient of the Pushcart Prize, his poems and short stories have appeared in POETRY, Electric Literature, Poem-a-Day, Best New Poets, The Threepenny Review, Zyzzyva, The Michigan Quarterly Review, Subtropics, Prairie Schooner, Beloit Poetry Journal, The Adroit Journal, The Missouri Review, and others. He earned his MFA in Poetry from Warren Wilson College, and his BA from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, where he was a 5-time recipient of the Hopwood Award.

Literary Arts Bridge
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, August 5, 2026

6 pm - 7 pm Publishing Talk: Catherine Tudish & Peter Orner Literary Arts Bridge (7 Lebanon St. Suite 105)

Born into an air force family, Catherine Tudish grew up moving from one place to the next, living in England and France, as well as both coasts and the heartland of America. After receiving a B.A. in English from Southern Illinois University and a Ph.D. in American Studies from St. Louis University, she began work as a teacher and journalist. Her first book, a collection of stories called Tenney’s Landing, was published by Scribner in 2005. Since then, she has published the novel American Cream, as well as numerous articles and essays in regional newspapers and magazines. Her latest book, A Thousand Souls: A Novel in Stories, will be published by Acre Books in April 2026. Catherine has taught writing and literature at George Washington University, Harvard, Dartmouth, and the Bread Loaf School of English. She lives in a village in central Vermont and teaches a community writing workshop at the local library.

Born in Chicago, Peter Orner is the author of eight acclaimed books, including The Gossip Columnist's Daughter; Maggie Brown & Others; Love and Shame and Love; Esther Stories, finalist for the PEN/Hemingway Award; and Am I Alone Here?, a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award. His work has appeared in The New Yorker, the Paris Review, The Believer, and The Best American Short Stories, and received four Pushcart Prizes. A former Guggenheim fellow and recipient of the Rome Prize from the American Academy of Arts and Letters, Orner is professor of Creative Writing at Dartmouth College. He lives with his family in Vermont, where he's also a volunteer firefighter.

Literary Arts Bridge
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00 pm Free Outdoor Concert – The Ferns Family Band Enfield Shaker Museum

2026 Free Outdoor Concert Series

The Ferns Family Band

Wednesday, August 5, 2026

Live music starts at 7 pm. Vending begins at 6 pm.

Join us outside the historic Stone Machine Shop for a FREE outdoor concert with The Ferns Family Band!

The Ferns Family Band is a trio fronted by award winning singer Mara Ferren, who never fails to impress with her seemingly effortless vocal prowess. Mara is supported by her brother Quinlan Ferren the backbone of the band on keys who masterfully juggles all piano, organ, bass, percussion and orchestral sounds. Father Justin Ferren, a seasoned musician spices up the spaces in between with tasty electric and acoustic guitar as well as percussion and live looping. The Ferns perform a mix of covers and originals spanning pop, folk, blues, soul and country sharing the magic of live music throughout New England and abroad.

Bring your chairs and blankets and enjoy this free outdoor concert with The Ferns Family Band at the Enfield Shaker Museum Stone Machine Shop.

Beer, wine, soda, and snacks will be available to purchase during the concert. (No private alcohol allowed.)

Enfield House of Pizza will be on site.

Live music will start at 7 pm. Vending will begin at 6 pm.

Rain or shine (we will move inside in case of rain.) Plenty of free parking.

THANK YOU TO OUR SPONSORS:

Aug 5 – Aug 26, 2026 Introduction to Wood Carving | 6-8 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Sessions start 6:00 pm · $220

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 8, 2026

9:00 am Yoga in the Garden Enfield Shaker Museum Garden

$15 – $20

August 8th at 9:00am
Yoga in the Garden

Join us on Saturday, August 8th at 9:00 a.m. for a peaceful and rejuvenating outdoor yoga experience. You will be  surrounded by nature and the tranquil atmosphere of the historic Shaker Village.  This is an all levels yoga class. This class is the perfect opportunity to slow down, reconnect, and begin your weekend with intention.

The class will be led by Caleigh, a Registered Yoga Teacher (RYT-500) and registered nurse. Caleigh brings a thoughtful and holistic approach to movement and wellness. Through mindful breathing, gentle stretching, and guided movement. Participants will be encouraged to cultivate balance, strength, flexibility, and relaxation. Whether you are new to yoga or have an established practice, this class will include modifications and options to help support your individual needs and comfort level.

This accessible yoga class is designed for participants who can comfortably get down to the ground and return to standing without assistance. Being able to stand unsupported, and bear weight on their hands, wrists, and knees will make this class more enjoyable.  Caleigh will provide guidance throughout the class to help participants move safely and confidently.

Please wear comfortable, stretchy clothing suitable for movement and bring a yoga mat or beach towel, along with water to stay hydrated. We also recommend arriving a few minutes early to settle into the garden setting and enjoy the peaceful surroundings before class begins.

In the event of inclement weather, the class will be moved indoors to the Stone Mill Building.

Come nurture your body, calm your mind, and enjoy a morning of wellness in one of the most beautiful settings in the Upper Valley.

Cost: $20 per person.- Non Member Pricce

15.00 Member Price

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Friday, August 14, 2026

5:30 pm Conversations | Natasha Wein: If These Feet Could Fly Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Free

This public event will take place in Natasha Wein's exhibition in the E.N. Wennberg Gallery, she will be speaking via Google Meets, Friday, August 14, 5:30-6:30 pm.

What do you do when the body you've built your art around stops working?

In 2019, Natasha Wein was diagnosed with Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome — a connective tissue disorder that ravaged her health, injured her spine, and took away the use of her hands. So she taped paper to the floor and learned to paint with her feet. Then, in 2024, while working on this very collection, she was struck by a car and sustained a traumatic brain injury. If These Feet Could Fly is what emerged anyway.

This collection of foot-made paintings doesn't just document survival — it asks the harder questions: How do you live a meaningful life when wounded beyond repair? How do you grieve who you used to be, and learn to love whoever you're becoming?

Through paint and paper on the floor, Natasha resurrects what remains, mourns what's gone, and lets her work reshape her into something whole — or at least, something legible.

Join us for an intimate evening with Natasha (via online with Google Meets) as she shares the story behind these paintings, the process of making them, and what it means to keep creating when everything keeps changing. This public event will take place in Natasha's exhibition in the E.N. Wennberg Gallery, she will be speaking via Google Meets.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, August 17, 2026

5:00 pm - 6:30 pm Kingdom of Devils: A Tale of Murder in the Shadow of the American Revolution Haldeman 041

The Department of History welcomes guest historian Katherine Grandjean (Wellesley College) to present a lecture on her recent monograph, Kingdom of Devils: A Tale of Murder in the Shadow of the American Revolution (Random House, 2026).

Kingdom of Devils tells the story of a brutal series of murders on the Revolutionary frontier. In the late 1790s, brothers Wiley and Micajah Harp killed dozens of people in the borderlands of Kentucky and Tennessee. No one has ever really explained why they did it. Drawing from her research, Grandjean uses the Harp murders to reveal the dark side of the young United States’ independence. Unspooling the mystery of the Harps’ spree, this talk exposes the hidden, violent legacies of the revolutionary era.

Katherine Grandjean is Associate Professor of History at Wellesley College. The recipient of major research fellowships from the National Endowment for the Humanities, the American Council of Learned Societies, and American Antiquarian Society, among others, she is also the author of American Passage (Harvard, 2015).

History Department
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, August 18, 2026

8 am - 9 am PhD Thesis Proposal: Emily Monroe academic Rm 035, ECSC

ZOOM LINK
Meeting ID: 921 5217 8697
Passcode: 105641

Abstract: Professional licensure asks engineers to hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public. Engineering education has strong tools to measure what students can do and how they identify as engineers, but it has no validated instrument for the normative professional commitment that licensure embodies: the felt obligation to protect the public even when doing so is costly. This dissertation develops and validates the Professional Engineering Commitments Scale (PECS), a measure grounded in Meyer and Allen's conception of normative commitment, to address that gap.

PECS measures four constructs: Public Welfare, Internalized Values, Licensure Commitment, and AI Verification and Accountability, the last capturing the engineer's responsibility to verify the work of increasingly capable AI systems and to remain the accountable human in the loop. Items were generated through a theory-, practice-, and standards-grounded pipeline and refined using semantic network analysis. A panel of practicing and academic experts then reviewed the pool, yielding strong content validity (S-CVI/Ave = 0.951) and a retained 66-item exploratory pool.

The proposed work tests the scale's structure and meaning. Exploratory factor analysis will examine dimensionality, followed by confirmatory factor analysis and tests of reliability and measurement invariance over the following year. The capstone design experience, studied as a site of cognitive apprenticeship and professional formation, provides the setting for examining whether and how commitment forms.

Finally, the dissertation positions commitment as a practically useful variable. By linking PECS scores to workforce questions in AI-integrated, cyber-resilient practice, it asks how a validated measure of professional commitment might inform where and how the next generation of engineers is developed and directed. The contribution is a theoretically grounded, empirically validated instrument that lets the field measure a commitment it has long valued but never directly assessed, along with a demonstration of why that measurement matters for public welfare.

Thesis Committee: Solomon Diamond (Chair), Geoffrey Parker, Eugene Santos Jr., Idalis Villanueva Alarcón (Department of Engineering Education, University of Florida)

Thayer School of Engineering

Full details ↗
Aug 18 Aug 19
7:00pm Last of the Granite State Rattlesnakes Alton Public Library 100 Main St. Alton NH 03809

Rattlesnakes were so common in colonial-era New Hampshire that dozens of places were named for them, ranging from the Massachusetts border to the White Mountains. They were so abundant some settlers could pay their property taxes with the bounties offered for killing them. Today, however, Crotalus horridus, the timber rattlesnake, is the state’s rarest native animal—only a handful are known to survive. State law protects them now, but that may not be enough to save them. Wildlife biologists have predicted this tiny remnant will die out without extraordinary intervention.

How did this feared and fascinating animal come to the very brink of extirpation in our state? This illustrated presentation maps how we got here through the social, political, and natural history of New Hampshire’s rattlesnakes. Historian and former newspaper reporter Dan Billin presents vivid, little-known stories and images from our centuries-long war on our rattlesnakes. Topics include who’s been bitten; the medicinal use of rattlesnake oil by the Shakers and others; the exploits of the “Rattlesnake King of New Hampshire”; the twentieth-century rattlesnake panic in the legislature; and the mysterious recent appearance of rattlesnakes with Pennsylvania genes. This program reviews what is and isn’t being done today to conserve our rattlesnakes, and the many obstacles to their survival.

There is parking on the street as well as a small parking lot. The entrance to the meeting room is in the back of the building at ground level.

Humanities to GoDan BillinAlton Historical SocietySandra Hammond 603-569-3745

Friday, August 21, 2026

6:00 pm Taboo Tour – August 21st Enfield Shaker Museum

$45 – $50

Taboo Tour

at the Enfield Shaker Museum

Step beyond the traditional history tour and explore the darker, more mysterious side of Shaker life. The Enfield Shaker Museum presents a special series of three Taboo Tours, each examining fascinating and controversial topics from Shaker history, including scandal, murder, spiritualism, the afterlife, and the enduring question: Were the Shakers a cult?
Each evening features a unique theme, an interactive hands-on activity, and an opportunity to uncover stories rarely told on standard tours. Guests will also enjoy one complimentary cocktail, beer, or glass of wine with their ticket, along with light refreshments.
Tour Dates:
July 31 6:00-8:00
August 21 6:00-8:00
October 30 6:00-8:00
Cost: $50 per person
Capacity: Limited to 20 participants per tour
Join us for an unforgettable evening of history, mystery, and conversation as we uncover the secrets, beliefs, and controversies hidden within the Enfield Shaker communit

Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 22, 2026

10:00 am Rubber Stamp Patch Printing | 10 am-1 pm AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

$0–$37.50

Instructor: Fairy Stewart

In this hands-on printmaking workshop, participants will carve original designs into erasers or rubber blocks, then use them to print custom patches onto fabric. No experience necessary! Participants will choose their carving block, sketch or draw a design directly onto it, and learn basic lino-cutting techniques to bring their image to life. Once carved, they'll select an ink color and print their design onto fabric scraps — with the option to run rubber block prints through a printing press for paper prints as well. Come ready to trade fabric scraps with fellow participants and leave with one-of-a-kind patches to sew, iron, or attach to a jacket, bag, or whatever you bring along.

Participants should bring: a garment or item to attach patches to. All other materials provided.

Finished patches will be heat-set or available for pickup the following day.

Wednesday, August 26, 2026

12 pm - 1 pm Online Info Session: Master of Energy Transition Online via Zoom

Accelerate Your Impact on the Clean Energy Transition

Register for the online Info Session

The world is building a cleaner, more resilient energy future — and that shift needs people who can bridge technology, business, and policy. Dartmouth’s Master of Energy Transition (MET) degree program is designed to train exactly those leaders.

Join us for a live online info session to see what makes the MET different, and find out if it's the right next step for you.

What to expect:

  • Meet the people behind the program — our faculty director, program director, and MET team
  • Learn about the MET faculty
  • Take an inside look at our interdisciplinary curriculum and project-based courses
  • Discover how we connect you with expert mentors through Dartmouth’s powerful alumni network
  • Get a preview of our career treks, where MET students meet energy leaders on the ground in Houston and San Francisco
  • Walk away with the full picture on applying — process, tuition, and financial aid


We'll close with a live Q&A, so bring your questions!

💡 Bonus: Attend the session, and we'll waive your MET application fee.

Save your spot and take the first step toward powering the future.

Register for the online Info Session

 

 

Arthur L. Irving Institute for Energy and Society

🎉 Join in person for:
• Real daubers
• Free popcorn
• Limited-edition lenticular stickers
• A chance to win a $20 Still North gift card

Each bingo square is a question you'll answer using the General Retention Schedule (GRS), Dartmouth's guide to records common across campus, which will be accessible during the session.

📋 Sample square: How long must waivers be retained before deletion? Four years.

There will also be some time to field questions about how the GRS applies to your work.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 29, 2026

9 am - 12 pm Gertrude Stein: Paris, Modernism, and a Family Connection Auditorium E, Dartmouth Hitchcock Medical Center One Medical Center Drive, Lebanon, NH 03766
Step into the legendary Paris salon of Gertrude Stein, where modern art was born and literary revolutionaries gathered. Nicholas Stein — documentary producer, author, and grandson of Gertrude Stein's favorite first cousins — brings his two-part presentation to Osher at Dartmouth after sold-out California engagements. Through family insights and cultural history, Nick will illuminate the woman who became one of the 20th century’s most influential figures. He’ll show how Gertrude’s radical experiments with language transformed literature, how her salon became the creative epicenter for Picasso, Hemingway, Matisse, and countless others, and why her fearless approach to art and life still resonates today. No background in modernist literature is needed for this presentation—just curiosity about a brilliant, unconventional woman who changed how we think about words, art, and creativity itself.

Nicholas Stein is a television producer (Border Wars, National Geographic; series for Discovery, PBS, Smithsonian, and A&E), author, and lifelong learning educator. Through a direct family connection — his grandparents were Gertrude Stein’s favorite first cousins — he brings extraordinary primary material to life, including rare 16mm home movies, personal correspondence, and stories passed down across three generations.

The presentation transports audiences inside the legendary Paris salon at 27 rue de Fleurus, where Picasso, Matisse, and Hemingway gathered, and explores Stein’s profound influence on both literature and the visual arts. As one attendee described it: “art history, biography, and intimate family memoir woven into one unforgettable experience.”

David Bisno, M.D. (Harvard, Dartmouth, Washington University School of Medicine) has led dynamic discussion programs for lifelong learners since 1993, across the U.S. and internationally.

Saturdays, August 29 and September 5
9:00 AM - 12:00 PM

Cost:
Osher Members: $65
Non-Members: $80

Learn More and Register

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth

Full details ↗
Sep 3 Sep 10 Sep 17 Sep 24 Oct 1 Oct 8

Friday, September 4, 2026

5:30 pm Conversations | Steve Arcone: Icescapes in the Abstract Main Gallery- AVA Gallery and Art Center, Lebanon

Free

Steve Arcone will be in the galleries talking about is work in the exhibition, Icescapes in the Abstract: Images from New Hampshire Ponds, Friday, September 4, 5:30-6:30 PM

Steve Arcone merges science and art by using landscape macro-photography to capture the beauty of small-scale natural scientific phenomena and thus, to evoke subjective interpretations of them. Steve seeks abstract-appearing structural compositions, usually containing clues to the actual setting for the unfamiliar viewer. Inspired by his career researching and subsurface imaging Antarctic and Alaskan snow and ice, his main year-to-year specialty is cyclically occurring pond ice undergoing change during mid fall to early spring. The compositional motifs he mainly seeks are star-like structures grown from ice seeds on vegetation, smaller scale structures within them, and their change under stress from sunlight, temperature changes and gravity. The blue, silver and grey tones of ice are often enhanced by their contrast with the enduring and underlying fall vegetative coloration. His other ongoing macro-imaging projects are woodland brook-bottom sediments as seen through flowing water, structures within spider web strands, and the laminar flow of oil on water.

Biography | Steve Arcone up in Greenwich Village in New York City, where he attended Elisabeth Irwin High School. He graduated from Cornell University with B.S. and M.E.E. degrees, and earned his PhD from Thayer School of Engineering at Dartmouth College. Steve is a research geophysicist, having retired from the Cold Regions Lab in Hanover, New Hampshire where he specialized in using ground-penetrating radar to image subsurface structures within permafrost, glaciers, ice sheets and lake bottom sediments, with many publications in leading scientific journals. His research has taken him to Antarctica 12 times and more than 40 times to Alaska. Steve has also been adjunct professor at Dartmouth College, and the Universities of Maine and Connecticut. Since retirement he has sought to merge science and art in his landscape photography, including macro-imaging of pond and brook ice, water and oil flow, and spider webs. Steve is a juried member of The League of New Hampshire Craftsmen and The New Hampshire Photographic Society. Since 2023 his images have appeared in ten group exhibitions (3 of which included 10 or more images) in New England and New York, Northern Woodlands and Smoky Quartz (2 issues) magazines and in the Geologic Society of America 2024 calendar. He regularly exhibits at the League summer fair at Sunapee Ski Resort in Sunapee, New Hampshire, and his work is on display at several of the League galleries.

Add to Google Calendar

Friday, September 11, 2026

7:30 pm - 8:30 pm Toni Dove: Sunjammer 6: A Tale Blown by a Solar Breeze Daryl Roth Studio Theater

All dates: Sep 11, Sep 12

Fall into a poetic, cosmic experience with this interactive journey through time, guided by the ghost of a Hellenistic astronomer.

A luminary and innovator in interactive stories, Dove is known for genre-mashing works that employ responsive technologies to create visceral narrative experiences and explore the implications of technological change. Her latest work braids together choreography, a dreamlike movie and a responsive machine that adapts to viewer or performer input.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Sep 11 Sep 12

Saturday, September 12, 2026

10:00am Duck Race Auburn Village 0

Thousands gather each September for a fun-filled family event hosted by the Auburn Historical Association. The Duck Race is the main attraction with prizes for the 10 fastest ducks, including a top prize of $1,000! There will be food trucks, local artisans and authors, a small petting farm, an apple pie contest, a kids baking contest, music and climbing wall. Active demonstrations of wool spinning and carding, cider pressing, blacksmithing, rug braiding, pottery making and so much more are ongoing continuously during the day. A parade to celebrate our country's 250th birthday will proceed down Hooksett Rd. 10 am to 3 pm in Auburn Village, Hooksett Road. Admission is free. For more info, visit www.auburnhistorical.org or contact us at info@auburnhistorical.org.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

7:30 pm - 9:00 pm Brian Stokes Mitchell Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

The Broadway legend takes us on a journey through the iconic parts he has never had the chance to sing… alongside some where he's been the star.

Two-time Tony winner. Baritone powerhouse. Best known as Coalhouse Walker in Ragtime and Don Quixote in Man of La Mancha, Stokes' career spans Broadway, jazz, the American songbook, film, concerts and more. Join us as he sails elegantly through the seas of these American musical traditions, and allow yourself to be swept away by one of the most versatile and in-demand singers.

Wednesday, September 16, 2026

10 am - 12 pm Tidy Your Inbox! The Deep Clean audience: Staff Zoom

Tackle your overflowing inbox — and then some! Delete even more emails while hearing expanded tips on Outlook settings, retention schedules, and managing ROT (Redundant, Obsolete, Trivial) content. In this longer, more in-depth session, we go further than ever to ensure compliance, help you feel less overwhelmed, and set yourself up for lasting inbox ease.

What to Expect:

  • Strategies and settings for newsletters, shared document notifications, task emails, event emails, and personal email
  • More time for bulk deleting overgrown inboxes (bring your worst)!
  • Time for your own questions too

Add to Google Calendar
5 pm - 6 pm Tocqueville on Liberal Democracy: Reflections on America's 250th Room 001, Rockefeller Center

Distinguished historian and political theorist Alan S. Kahan, a PEP visiting professor in the Government department (Fall term) and leading Tocqueville expert, will reflect on how the author of Democracy in America conceived of liberal democracy, and what we might learn from him today. 

Political Economy Project
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm The First Amendment Moultonborough Public Library 4 Holland St Moultonborough NH 03254

The First Amendment protects our most basic freedoms, none more important than freedom of speech. But what do we do about speech that threatens to destroy the social fabric? This presentation considers the constitutional arguments for and against hate speech codes and why the Supreme Court ruled against St. Paul's hate speech ordinance. If hate speech codes are unconstitutional, it falls on citizens to find other ways to counter hateful speech. We'll explore what capacities citizens need to preserve freedom and the social fabric. Could it be that persuasion and deliberation might be better strategies for all of us? 

Humanities to GoMeg MottMoultonborough Public LibraryJosianne Fitzgerald adultservices@moultonboroughlibrary.org

3 pm - 3 pm 26F OLE Application Deadline audience: Students-Undergraduate Online Application

Through Outdoor Leadership Experience (OLE), Dartmouth mentors facilitate outdoor programming with middle school students from Canaan, NH and Hartford, VT. Mentors work with youth to develop leadership and outdoor skills in weekly after-school adventures throughout the year, building experiences and skills in the outdoors that culminates in an overnight camping trip in the spring.

The application for Fall 2026 will be posted by the end of August.

3 pm - 3 pm 26F Growing Change Application Deadline audience: Students-Undergraduate Online Application

3 pm - 3 pm 26F Dartmouth Teach Application Deadline audience: Students-Undergraduate Online Application

In Dartmouth Teach, Dartmouth students serve as teaching assistants to support elementary and middle school teachers and youth, gaining classroom experience in this academic assistance program. Dartmouth students in Teach support general education (K-2) and art (K-5) classes at Dothan Brook Elementary School, and may have the opportunity to support local middle school classrooms.

Add to Google Calendar
3 pm - 3 pm 26F SEAD Application Deadline audience: Students-Undergraduate Online Application

Strengthening Educational Access with Dartmouth (SEAD) connects Dartmouth undergrads with high school students from local communities, supporting the high school students year-round in understanding college, preparing for it, identifying good-fit colleges, applying to those schools, and thriving once they enroll!

 

Dartmouth mentors, or Educational Access Advisors (EAAs), support high school students in either after-school sessions or midday sessions at local schools throughout the year.

 

The application for Fall 2026 will be posted by the end of August.
